description: Unvalidated LLM finding outputs before evidence validation and filtering.

finding.summary: The rom2 module allows unrestricted AXI-bus writes to overwrite key material stored in secure_reg, including JTAG authentication keys and access-control registers, with no privilege or lock check guarding the write path.

evidence.description: rom2.secure_reg holds all key material: AES keys, a 192-bit JTAG key, and two access-control bitmaps. On reset, these are copied from the hardcoded mem constant.
evidence.supports_claim: secure_reg is the root of the permission chain and stores privileged key and access-control data.

evidence.description: The always_ff write path (lines 36-41) gates writes only on req_i && we_i with no privilege-level check, no lock bit, and no address-range restriction beyond the RomSize index. Any AXI master that can assert req_i and we_i can overwrite any entry in secure_reg, including the JTAG key at index 1 and access-control entries at indices 2-3.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing privilege/lock guard on the write path allows untrusted AXI masters to corrupt key and access-control registers.

evidence.description: The rom2_fuse AXI slave port is wired directly to rom2 through an axi2mem bridge with no interposing access filter. rom2_we is passed straight to rom2.we_i, making the unguarded write path reachable from the AXI fabric.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI interface provides an unfiltered write entry point into rom2.

evidence.description: Lines 215-217 continuously assign jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg directly from secure_reg slots 1, 2, and 3. A successful write to secure_reg immediately affects the exported permission signals.
evidence.supports_claim: Overwriting secure_reg propagates instantly to the key and access-control outputs consumed by the rest of the SoC.

evidence.description: The access_ctrl matrix consumed by the AXI crossbar is derived entirely from access_ctrl_reg with no additional validation or immutability enforcement. There is no secondary check that would detect or reject a tampered value.
evidence.supports_claim: The access-control enforcement layer trusts the raw value of access_ctrl_reg, so overwriting secure_reg directly defeats peripheral access control.

evidence.description: jtag_key (32 bits of the JTAG key) and access_ctrl_reg (two 48-bit access-control words) are declared as plain logic signals in ariane_testharness with no protection or one-time-programmable semantics, confirming that corrupted values flow into JTAG authentication and crossbar permission decisions.
evidence.supports_claim: An attacker who can issue AXI writes to the rom2_fuse slave can replace JTAG keys and escalate or block peripheral access for any master.

finding.reasoning_summary: rom2 is described as a fuse-replication store for sensitive keys and access-control bitmaps. On reset it copies hardcoded mem values into secure_reg. However, the always_ff block at line 40 allows arbitrary writes to any secure_reg entry via the AXI bus whenever req_i and we_i are asserted. There is no privilege-level check, no write-once/lock bit, and no address filtering beyond a simple RomSize bound. The write path is directly reachable from the rom2_fuse AXI slave through an axi2mem bridge. After reset, any AXI master with access to the rom2_fuse address range can overwrite JTAG keys (slot 1) or access-control bitmaps (slots 2-3), immediately changing the signals that gate JTAG authentication and crossbar peripheral access control.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI master reachable from the rom2_fuse slave can (1) replace the JTAG key to lock out or impersonate legitimate debuggers, and (2) rewrite access-control registers to grant itself access to any peripheral or deny access to legitimate masters, completely bypassing the intended hardware permission model.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The AXI address map and master-privilege assignment for the rom2_fuse slave are not shown, so the exact set of masters that can reach the write path is unconfirmed. However, the absence of any guard in the RTL itself is clear from the source snippets.

finding.summary: Disabled peripherals (SPI and Ethernet) return hardcoded sentinel data 0xdeadbeef on read channels and always accept transactions with SLVERR, with no access gating — any master can read and the response data leaks a fixed magic value rather than being suppressed or zeroed.

evidence.description: When InclSPI=0, the SPI slave is not truly disabled: it asserts aw_ready, ar_ready, and w_ready unconditionally (lines 772-774), responds to every write with RESP_SLVERR (line 778) and to every read with RESP_SLVERR plus r_data='hdeadbeef (lines 782-783). Any initiator can issue transactions to the SPI address range and receive a predictable, non-zero data response with no gating whatsoever.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing access guard on disabled peripheral: transactions are accepted and a fixed sentinel value is returned instead of being blocked or zeroed.

evidence.description: When InclEthernet=0, identical behaviour is present for the Ethernet slave: aw_ready, ar_ready, w_ready are permanently asserted (lines 961-963), writes respond with RESP_SLVERR (line 967), and reads return r_data='hdeadbeef with RESP_SLVERR (lines 971-972). The peripheral slot is fully accessible with no privilege or lifecycle guard.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing access guard on disabled peripheral: transactions are accepted and a fixed sentinel value is returned instead of being blocked or zeroed.

evidence.description: The testharness instantiates ariane_peripherals with InclUART=0, InclSPI=0, InclEthernet=0, meaning all three peripheral slots enter the stub path that unconditionally accepts and responds to AXI transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: The vulnerable stub paths are actively exercised with all optional peripherals disabled.

evidence.description: The module port list shows AXI_BUS.in interfaces for spi and ethernet are exposed to any connected master with no privilege signal, user-field check, or enable qualifier in the port interface itself.
evidence.supports_claim: Peripheral AXI slave ports lack any permission/privilege checking at the interface boundary.

finding.reasoning_summary: When optional peripherals (SPI, Ethernet) are excluded via parameters, their AXI slave stubs permanently assert ready signals and respond to every transaction. Read responses return 'hdeadbeef rather than being suppressed. There is no guard that rejects transactions, checks an access-control register, or requires elevated privilege. Any AXI master routed to these address regions can freely issue reads and writes that complete with SLVERR but with data leakage of the sentinel value. In a security-sensitive SoC this constitutes both a missing access guard and unintended sensitive readback behavior on disabled hardware.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software agent mapped to the disabled peripheral address ranges can probe them, receive successful handshakes (SLVERR is still a completed transaction), and read a predictable non-zero value. This can be used for address-space reconnaissance, and the always-ready write channel could interact unexpectedly with any downstream logic that is later enabled or patched in. In a testbench context this also masks coverage of proper denial behavior.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The address map that routes masters to these peripheral slots is not shown in the provided snippets, so it cannot be confirmed whether an upstream address decoder prevents unauthorized masters from reaching these AXI slaves in the production configuration. The finding is clearly evidenced within the peripheral module itself.

finding.summary: Disabled peripheral stubs (SPI and Ethernet) respond to all AXI transactions immediately with ready=1 and return 'hdeadbeef as read data, silently accepting all writes and leaking a fixed sentinel value on reads, with no access control or error gating beyond a SLVERR response code.

evidence.description: When InclSPI=0, the else-branch unconditionally asserts spi.aw_ready=1, spi.ar_ready=1, spi.w_ready=1, immediately accepts every write/read transaction, and returns spi.r_data='hdeadbeef on every read. No access check or privilege guard is present.
evidence.supports_claim: Any bus master can issue AXI transactions to the SPI address region and receive a well-formed (though SLVERR-flagged) response with data 0xdeadbeef, meaning write transactions are silently swallowed and read transactions leak a fixed value without any permission enforcement.

evidence.description: When InclEthernet=0, the else-branch unconditionally asserts ethernet.aw_ready=1, ethernet.ar_ready=1, ethernet.w_ready=1, and returns ethernet.r_data='hdeadbeef on every read. Same pattern as SPI stub: no guard, no privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: Any bus master can issue AXI transactions to the Ethernet address region and receive well-formed responses with data 0xdeadbeef, bypassing any intended access restriction on the disabled peripheral.

evidence.description: The testharness instantiates ariane_peripherals with InclUART=0, InclSPI=0, InclEthernet=0, meaning all three peripheral stubs are active and their unguarded AXI stub logic is reachable from the master bus.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms that the stub branches with missing guards are actually enabled in the testbench configuration.

evidence.description: ariane_peripherals exposes AXI_BUS.in ports for spi, ethernet, gpio, plic, aes, rom2_fuse, and uart without any top-level privilege or lock signal gating access to these ports.
evidence.supports_claim: The peripheral module's interface has no permission input to gate access; all protection must be internal, and the disabled-peripheral stubs provide none.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets (SNIP-0005 and SNIP-0006) show that when SPI and Ethernet peripherals are excluded via their compile-time parameters, the generate-else stubs immediately accept every incoming AXI transaction (ready=1 combinatorially), silently consume all writes, and return the fixed sentinel value 0xdeadbeef on all reads while signalling SLVERR. There is no privilege check, no lock/auth guard, and no filtering of the master identity before responding. SNIP-0008 confirms these stubs are active in the testharness build. Although this is testbench code, the same ariane_peripherals module is instantiated in the FPGA flow (SNIP-0002), where the same stub behavior would apply to any excluded peripheral, allowing any AXI master to interact with the stub address space without restriction.
finding.security_impact: A bus master with access to the SPI or Ethernet address regions can silently write data that is accepted and discarded (masking errors), and read back the fixed value 0xdeadbeef. More critically, the immediate ready=1 response means no transaction is ever stalled or rejected at the permission level; software assuming a SLVERR response implies 'access denied' may incorrectly infer the peripheral is protected when it is actually an open stub. In an FPGA deployment this could mask misconfigured address maps or allow privilege-escalation via unexpected AXI responses.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The ariane_peripherals file is under tb/ suggesting it may be testbench-only; however SNIP-0002 shows it is also instantiated in fpga/src/ariane_xilinx.sv. It is unclear whether the FPGA build uses a different, hardened implementation. No snippet shows the GPIO port stub or any runtime privilege/lock register that might gate access at a higher level.

finding.summary: The JTAG authentication `pass` register is only loaded from `jtag_key` during reset (active-low reset asserted), and `pass_chk` is a combinational variable that is never registered, meaning the authentication state is lost every clock cycle and the password check can be trivially bypassed or is functionally broken.

evidence.description: The `pass` register is assigned from `jtag_key` only when `~rst_ni` is asserted (i.e., during reset). Outside of reset, `pass` retains its last value, but critically it is only updated during the reset condition, meaning in normal operation the password value is whatever was captured at reset time.
evidence.supports_claim: The `pass` register is loaded during reset, but `pass_chk` is a combinational signal (declared without `reg` semantics in the always_comb block) that is not retained across cycles.

evidence.description: At line 78, `pass_chk` is declared as a plain `logic` variable. Within the `always_comb` block it is used to gate read access (`pass_chk == 1'b1` required for DTM_READ at line 110) and to set `umode_o`. However, `pass_chk` is never assigned a default value at the top of the `always_comb` block (lines 93-99 only default `error_dmi_busy`, `state_d`, `address_d`, `data_d`, `error_d`, `dmi_req_valid`). This means `pass_chk` infers a latch or has an undefined default, making the authentication gate unreliable.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing default assignment for `pass_chk` in the always_comb block means its value is latched from a previous evaluation context, which is a functional bug that undermines the authentication guard on JTAG read access.

evidence.description: At line 112-113, the `DTM_WRITE` path (`dm::DTM_WRITE`) does NOT check `pass_chk`. Any JTAG write operation proceeds unconditionally to the `Write` state without requiring authentication. Only `DTM_READ` requires `pass_chk == 1'b1`. This allows an unauthenticated attacker to perform arbitrary debug module writes via JTAG.
evidence.supports_claim: Write access to the debug module interface is not gated by the JTAG key authentication (`pass_chk`), bypassing the intended authentication mechanism for write operations.

evidence.description: rom2 stores cryptographic keys in `secure_reg` and exposes them via `rdata_o`. The `secure_reg` is writable via AXI at line 40 (`secure_reg[...] <= wdata_i`) without any access control check within the module itself, relying entirely on external bus-level protection.
evidence.supports_claim: The key store (rom2) allows writes to `secure_reg` based solely on `req_i` and `we_i` with no privilege check, meaning any bus master that can reach the rom2 AXI port can overwrite keys.

evidence.description: At line 215, `jtag_key` is directly assigned as `key_reg_out[1][31:0]`, which is the lower 32 bits of the JTAG key from rom2's `secure_reg[1]`. This 32-bit value is the authentication reference for JTAG access, and it flows from a writable register in rom2.
evidence.supports_claim: The JTAG authentication key originates from a writable rom2 register, so an attacker who can write to rom2 can set the JTAG key to a known value and trivially pass authentication.

evidence.description: In the FPGA instantiation (`ariane_xilinx`), `dmi_jtag` is instantiated without a `jtag_key` port connection, meaning the key input is undriven (floating/zero) in the FPGA build, effectively nullifying the authentication mechanism on FPGA targets.
evidence.supports_claim: The FPGA instantiation of dmi_jtag does not connect the `jtag_key` port, so the password defaults to zero and authentication is trivially satisfied by presenting a zero password.

finding.reasoning_summary: Multiple compounding vulnerabilities exist in the JTAG authentication chain: (1) Write operations via JTAG bypass `pass_chk` entirely; (2) `pass_chk` is a combinational logic variable with no registered state and missing default assignment, making the authentication state unreliable across cycles; (3) The `jtag_key` is sourced from a writable register in rom2, allowing key corruption; (4) The FPGA instantiation omits the `jtag_key` port connection, leaving authentication effectively disabled on FPGA. These issues collectively mean the JTAG debug module interface can be accessed without valid authentication.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG physical access or AXI bus access can: bypass the JTAG authentication to perform arbitrary debug module writes (full system compromise via debug module); corrupt the JTAG key in rom2 to enable authentication bypass; on FPGA targets, trivially authenticate with a zero key. This enables full debug access including memory read/write and CPU control.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact synthesis behavior of the undefaulted `pass_chk` in always_comb (latch vs. undefined) depends on tool settings. The FPGA snippet (SNIP-0002) does not show all port connections so there is a small possibility `jtag_key` is connected later in the file beyond the shown range.

finding.summary: The rom2 module allows unrestricted write access to its secure_reg (key store) via the we_i interface, and on reset unconditionally copies all keys including the JTAG authentication key from the fuse-like mem array into the readable/writable secure_reg, which is then exposed as an output port and forwarded to dmi_jtag as the authentication credential.

evidence.description: rom2.secure_reg holds all cryptographic keys including the JTAG key (index 1) and access-control credentials (indices 2 and 3). The mem array is declared as const but secure_reg is a writable register output.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material is stored in a register that can be overwritten by any AXI master via the we_i path without any privilege check.

evidence.description: The write path at line 40 is: if(req_i) begin if (!we_i) begin ... end else begin secure_reg[addr_i[...]] <= wdata_i; end end. There is no privilege level check, no lock bit, and no authentication guard before allowing writes to secure_reg.
evidence.supports_claim: Any bus master with access to the rom2_fuse AXI slave can overwrite any key in secure_reg, including the JTAG authentication key.

evidence.description: On reset (~rst_ni), secure_reg <= mem copies all four key slots from the hardcoded const mem array into the live writable registers. There is no one-time-programmable lock or write-once semantic; after reset the registers remain freely writable.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset correctly initializes the key registers but provides no subsequent write protection, leaving them mutable for the entire operational lifetime.

evidence.description: ariane_peripherals instantiates rom2 and connects rom2_we directly from the AXI-to-memory bridge output, meaning any AXI transaction with write intent reaches the key store without interception.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI slave interface for rom2_fuse provides a direct write path into secure_reg.

evidence.description: Lines 215-217 expose key_reg_out[1][31:0] as jtag_key and key_reg_out[2/3][47:0] as access_ctrl_reg with no masking or hardening. Because secure_reg is writable, an attacker can overwrite key_reg_out[1] to inject an arbitrary JTAG password.
evidence.supports_claim: Writable key registers are directly wired to security-critical outputs, allowing an attacker to substitute the JTAG authentication credential.

evidence.description: dmi_jtag copies jtag_key into the internal pass register on reset (pass <= jtag_key). Because the JTAG key originates from the writable secure_reg, an attacker who overwrites secure_reg[1] before or during a reset can control the JTAG authentication secret.
evidence.supports_claim: Injection of an attacker-controlled value into secure_reg[1] propagates to the JTAG authentication pass register, allowing unauthorized debug access.

evidence.description: access_ctrl_reg (derived from writable secure_reg[2] and secure_reg[3]) is used to populate the AXI crossbar access_ctrl matrix that enforces peripheral-level privilege. Overwriting these registers removes or modifies access controls for all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: Corrupting secure_reg[2/3] disables or bypasses the AXI crossbar access-control policy for all masters and peripherals.

finding.reasoning_summary: rom2 is designed as a fuse-like key store but implements a fully writable register file. After reset copies keys from the immutable mem array into secure_reg, any AXI master that can address the rom2_fuse slave can issue write transactions (we_i=1) to overwrite any key slot. No privilege check, lock register, or write-once semantic guards the write path. The corrupted keys are then immediately visible on the output ports: jtag_key feeds the JTAG authentication credential in dmi_jtag, and access_ctrl_reg feeds the AXI crossbar permission matrix. Both security properties can therefore be completely subverted by a software-level or AXI-capable attacker.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access to the rom2_fuse peripheral can (1) replace the JTAG authentication key to gain unauthorized hardware debug access, and (2) overwrite access-control registers to grant or revoke any master's access to any peripheral, bypassing the entire AXI crossbar permission system.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet for dmi_jtag does not show the full authentication comparison logic that uses pass, so it is not fully confirmed that controlling pass alone is sufficient to bypass JTAG authentication without other factors. Additionally, the AXI crossbar implementation (axi_node_intf_wrap) enforcement logic is not in the provided snippets, so the exact effect of corrupted access_ctrl values on enforcement is inferred rather than directly observed.

finding.summary: rom2 exposes cryptographic keys and access-control registers with no privilege or access-control guard: any bus master can read all keys via rdata_o, and any master can overwrite secure_reg contents via a write transaction, bypassing the ROM's intended read-only key-storage semantics.

evidence.description: rom2 stores four 192-bit secret values: an AES key, a JTAG key, and two access-control master bitmaps in the 'mem' constant array, copied into 'secure_reg' on reset. The comment explicitly states 'ROM2: Which have all the keys.'
evidence.supports_claim: The module holds highly sensitive cryptographic and access-control material that requires strict read/write protection.

evidence.description: Line 40: 'secure_reg[addr_i[$clog2(RomSize)-1+3:3]] <= wdata_i;' executes whenever req_i is asserted and we_i is high, with no privilege level, lock register, or authentication check. Any bus master that can issue an AXI write to the rom2_fuse address range can overwrite any of the four 192-bit key slots.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing write-protection guard allows arbitrary overwrite of cryptographic keys and access-control registers.

evidence.description: Line 47: 'assign rdata_o = (raddr_q < RomSize) ? secure_reg[raddr_q] : 0;' unconditionally outputs the full 192-bit key value to any requester. No privilege check, TrustZone attribution, or read-lock guards this path.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive readback: all 192-bit key material is readable by any unprivileged bus master.

evidence.description: ariane_peripherals instantiates rom2 and wires rom2_we and rom2_addr directly from an axi2mem converter (lines 196-212) with no interposed access-control logic. The 'secure_reg' output port (key_reg_out) is also exposed at the peripheral level without restriction.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI bus interface provides unrestricted read and write access to the rom2 key storage.

evidence.description: The ariane_peripherals module declaration (lines 12-55) shows no privilege-level input, no secure/non-secure attribution signal, and no write-lock port connected to rom2. The rom2_fuse AXI slave is exposed identically to other peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: No guard logic exists at the peripheral integration level to restrict access to rom2.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni low), secure_reg is loaded from mem (line 34). After reset, we_i-controlled writes can silently replace any key entry without any lock or one-time-programmable semantic, defeating the 'fuse replication' intent stated in the comment.
evidence.supports_claim: Post-reset writability of fuse-replicated keys violates expected lifecycle immutability.

finding.reasoning_summary: rom2 is described as holding cryptographic keys (AES, JTAG) and access-control bitmaps replicated from fuses. The RTL (SNIP-0003) shows that after reset any bus master can both read the full 192-bit key values through rdata_o and overwrite any key slot through the write path (we_i + wdata_i), with no privilege check, lock register, or authentication gate at any level of the hierarchy (rom2 itself, ariane_peripherals, or the top-level instantiation). This constitutes both a sensitive-readback vulnerability and a missing write-protection vulnerability on security-critical state.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI bus master can (1) read all cryptographic keys (AES, JTAG) and access-control policies in a single burst, enabling key theft or policy discovery, and (2) overwrite any key or access-control entry to escalate privileges, deny service, or backdoor authenticated operations controlled by those keys.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed from the snippets alone whether the AXI address decoder in the SoC restricts which masters can reach the rom2_fuse slave port; however, the absence of any guard inside rom2 or ariane_peripherals means the protection, if any, relies entirely on external address-space partitioning with no defense-in-depth.

finding.summary: rom2 secure key registers are writable by any bus master without any privilege or lock check, and sensitive key material is exposed via output ports with no access control.

evidence.description: The rom2 module stores cryptographic keys (AES key, JTAG key, access control keys) in 'secure_reg', a 4x192-bit register array initialized from fuse-like constants on reset. The comment explicitly labels these as security-critical.
evidence.supports_claim: These registers contain sensitive key material that should be protected from unauthorized read or write access.

evidence.description: At line 40, the always_ff block allows any requester to write directly to secure_reg entries via 'secure_reg[addr_i[...]] <= wdata_i' when req_i=1 and we_i=1. There is no privilege check, no lock bit, no write-once protection, and no authentication before the write is accepted.
evidence.supports_claim: Any bus master with access to the rom2_fuse AXI port can overwrite cryptographic keys stored in secure_reg.

evidence.description: The only condition guarding writes is 'if(req_i) begin if (!we_i) ... else secure_reg[...] <= wdata_i'. There is no additional privilege level check, no write-protection register, and no one-time-programmable enforcement.
evidence.supports_claim: The missing guard confirms no access control exists for write operations to the key store.

evidence.description: secure_reg is declared as an output port (line 13: 'output logic [3:0][191:0] secure_reg') exposing all 4 x 192-bit key values continuously to the parent module without any read gating or masking.
evidence.supports_claim: Full key material is permanently readable by any logic connected to the secure_reg output, with no read access control.

evidence.description: In ariane_peripherals, the secure_reg output is assigned to key_reg_out and then portions are directly assigned to jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg outputs (lines 215-217) without any access gate, making key material visible to downstream modules unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material flows from secure_reg to module output ports without protection, enabling readback by any observer of these signals.

evidence.description: On reset (~rst_ni), secure_reg is loaded from the hardcoded 'mem' constant array (line 34: secure_reg <= mem). After reset, the register contents can be freely overwritten via the write path. There is no mechanism to re-lock or protect the registers post-initialization.
evidence.supports_claim: Keys are writable throughout normal operation, not just during a provisioning lifecycle phase.

evidence.description: The rom2_fuse interface is an AXI bus port (AXI_BUS.in rom2_fuse) converted through axi2mem to produce rom2_req, rom2_we, rom2_addr, rom2_wdata. Any AXI transaction to this region translates directly to a req+we to rom2 with no interposing permission check.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI-accessible entry point provides an unrestricted path to write the secure key registers.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rom2 module holds cryptographic key material (AES, JTAG, access-control keys) in secure_reg. The write path (req_i && we_i) accepts writes from any bus master without privilege verification, lock protection, or write-once semantics. Additionally, the entire secure_reg content is output as a plain port that is unconditionally wired to module-level output signals (jtag_key, access_ctrl_reg), enabling sensitive readback. The combination of unguarded writes and unprotected sensitive readback constitutes a concrete permission-security vulnerability.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access to the rom2_fuse region can: (1) overwrite AES keys, JTAG keys, or access-control keys, undermining all security policies enforced by those keys; (2) read back the full 768-bit key material through the unconditionally exposed secure_reg/key_reg_out output ports, enabling key extraction.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The upstream AXI interconnect access-control configuration (e.g., whether a firewall or MPU restricts which masters can reach rom2_fuse) is not visible in the provided snippets. However, within the RTL of rom2 and ariane_peripherals themselves, there is no access control, which is sufficient to establish the vulnerability regardless of any external bus-level protection.

finding.summary: ROM storing security-critical access control keys accepts an AXI write data path (wdata_i) without any visible write-protection guard, enabling unauthorized modification of fuse-replicated permission data.

evidence.description: rom2 stores access-control key/permission data described as a 'replication of fuse' in a const mem array, with secure_reg output carrying 4x192-bit key values used for peripheral access control decisions.
evidence.supports_claim: The memory inside rom2 holds security-critical permission data that should be immutable after manufacturing/boot.

evidence.description: The rom2 module exposes both we_i (write enable) and wdata_i (64-bit write data) input ports. No lock register, lifecycle check, privilege check, or any other guard logic is visible in the module interface to prevent writes after initialization.
evidence.supports_claim: A ROM storing fuse-replicated security keys accepts arbitrary write data without write-protection, violating the immutability requirement of such storage.

evidence.description: An axi2mem bridge directly drives rom2_wdata and rom2_we signals into the rom2 instance. Any AXI master that can reach this address region can assert we_i and supply arbitrary wdata_i to overwrite the access-control keys.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI bus provides an unconstrained write path into the ROM, making the missing write guard exploitable by any bus master.

evidence.description: The signal declarations for rom2_we and rom2_wdata show no accompanying lock or permission signal; the we_o of axi2mem flows directly to rom2 we_i with no interposing protection logic.
evidence.supports_claim: Absence of any guard signal between the AXI bridge output and the ROM write port confirms the missing protection.

finding.reasoning_summary: rom2 is described as a fuse-replication ROM holding access-control permission keys for peripheral masters. Despite its security-critical role, the module has a fully functional write interface (we_i, wdata_i) driven directly from an AXI-to-memory bridge with no observable write-lock, lifecycle state check, or privilege filter between the bus and the ROM write port. Any AXI master capable of addressing the rom2 region can overwrite the permission keys at runtime, subverting the entire access-control policy the keys enforce.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or compromised bus master can overwrite the fuse-replicated access-control keys stored in rom2, granting or revoking peripheral access permissions arbitrarily. This undermines the hardware-enforced permission model for all peripherals protected by these keys, potentially enabling privilege escalation or denial of service across the SoC.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of rom2.sv beyond line 20 is not shown, so it is possible that write blocking is implemented internally (e.g., ignoring we_i despite accepting it). The axi2mem module internals and any system-level address filtering are also not provided. However, the exposed write port with no visible guard and the direct AXI bridge connection constitute a credible vulnerability based on available evidence.

finding.summary: Access control registers derived directly from raw key_reg_out fuse memory without any authentication, locking, or integrity check, allowing unguarded propagation of privilege-level access control bits to the AXI crossbar.

evidence.description: access_ctrl_reg[0] and access_ctrl_reg[1] are assigned directly from key_reg_out[2][47:0] and key_reg_out[3][47:0] via combinational continuous assigns with no guard, lock, or validity check.
evidence.supports_claim: The permission state is set from raw fuse/ROM data with no protection or verification step.

evidence.description: key_reg_out is declared as a 4-entry 192-bit wide signal sourced from a secure_reg output of the axi2rom2 instance, representing fuse/ROM-stored key and access control material.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive security-critical data (keys and access control configuration) flows directly from fuse ROM to the access control state without any interposing guard.

evidence.description: access_ctrl[i][j] is assigned by slicing access_ctrl_reg[i][4*j +: 4] in a generate loop with no conditional check on privilege level, lifecycle state, or any authentication signal. The processor privilege level (priv_lvl_processor) is captured but not used to gate the access_ctrl derivation.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard logic preventing the access control matrix from being populated or read before a valid security state is established.

evidence.description: The access_ctrl signal, which directly controls AXI crossbar peripheral access permissions per privilege level, is derived combinationally from fuse data with no reset-state default, no lock register, and no authentication check. Any reset or fuse misconfiguration immediately propagates to all peripheral access permissions.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard/lock on permission state update means the AXI crossbar access control can be in an undefined or attacker-influenced state at reset or during fuse loading.

evidence.description: The ariane_peripherals instance connects access_ctrl_reg output directly to the testharness signal of the same name, which then feeds the AXI crossbar access control matrix without any intervening validation.
evidence.supports_claim: The full chain from fuse ROM output to AXI crossbar permission enforcement is purely combinational with no protection state verification.

finding.reasoning_summary: The access control matrix governing which privilege levels can access which AXI peripherals is derived by combinationally slicing raw fuse/ROM key register outputs (key_reg_out) with no authentication, no lock register, no lifecycle gating, and no reset-safe default. The processor's privilege level is read but never used to gate the derivation of access_ctrl. If the fuse data is uninitialized, corrupted, or influenced by an attacker (e.g., via the AXI bus to the rom2_fuse interface), the access control permissions for all peripherals would be compromised. There is no evidence of any guard, handshake, or integrity check between the fuse readout and the application of permissions to the AXI crossbar.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can influence the rom2_fuse AXI interface or who operates during a window when fuse data is not yet valid can set arbitrary access_ctrl values, potentially granting unprivileged software (user-mode) access to privileged peripherals, or denying legitimate access, bypassing the entire privilege-level enforcement mechanism at the AXI crossbar.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full implementation of the secure_reg output from the ROM/fuse module is not shown, so it is unclear whether additional protection exists inside that module. The axi_node_intf_wrap crossbar implementation is not shown, so the exact enforcement semantics are unknown. These files are testbench (tb/) files, which may reduce real-world impact if this is simulation-only infrastructure, but the pattern represents a genuine missing-guard vulnerability.

finding.summary: The rom2 secure register storing cryptographic keys and access control policies is directly writable by any bus master via the AXI interface without any privilege or authentication check, allowing unprivileged software to overwrite JTAG keys and access control permissions at runtime.

evidence.description: rom2.secure_reg holds all key material and access control bitmaps. On reset it is loaded from the hardcoded mem[] array. However, the always_ff block also accepts writes unconditionally: whenever req_i is asserted and we_i is high, secure_reg[addr] is updated with wdata_i with no privilege, lock, or authentication check.
evidence.supports_claim: The register storing sensitive key material can be overwritten through a normal bus write transaction.

evidence.description: mem[] contains hardcoded JTAG keys (locations 0-1) and access control bitmaps for two masters (locations 2-3), making secure_reg the single authoritative source of all permission state in the system.
evidence.supports_claim: The content of secure_reg directly controls both authentication keys and peripheral access permissions.

evidence.description: The rom2 instance is exposed through a full AXI slave interface (rom2_fuse port), bridged via axi2mem. The axi2mem bridge converts AXI transactions directly to req_i/we_i/addr_i/wdata_i signals passed to rom2 without any interposing access gate.
evidence.supports_claim: Any AXI master that can reach the rom2_fuse bus segment can issue write transactions to secure_reg.

evidence.description: Lines 215-217 directly assign jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg from key_reg_out (which is secure_reg) with no conditional guard, no lock bit check, and no write-protection logic between the AXI path and the key register output.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard logic preventing a modified secure_reg from propagating immediately to jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg outputs.

evidence.description: access_ctrl[i][j] is directly sliced from access_ctrl_reg, which is driven by the writable secure_reg. This access_ctrl array is passed to the AXI crossbar to enforce peripheral access permissions for all bus masters.
evidence.supports_claim: An attacker who overwrites secure_reg can set arbitrary access control permissions for any peripheral and any master.

evidence.description: Line 40 shows 'secure_reg[addr_i[...]] <= wdata_i;' executing unconditionally whenever req_i && we_i, with no privilege-level check, no write-once latch, no lock register, and no authentication. This is the concrete missing guard.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing write protection on the security-critical register constitutes the core vulnerability.

evidence.description: access_ctrl_reg is declared in the testharness and wired to the AXI crossbar access control logic. Its value is derived entirely from the writable rom2 secure_reg, so overwriting secure_reg directly subverts all peripheral access control enforcement.
evidence.supports_claim: The vulnerability allows full bypass of the peripheral access control policy enforced by the AXI crossbar.

finding.reasoning_summary: rom2 is designed as a fuse-like key store that initialises secure_reg from a hardcoded mem[] array on reset. However, the same always_ff block that performs reset-time initialisation also accepts runtime writes whenever req_i and we_i are asserted, with absolutely no privilege, lock, or authentication guard. The rom2 is exposed through an unrestricted AXI slave port (rom2_fuse). The key_reg_out output (connected to secure_reg) is directly wired to jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg without any protection. access_ctrl_reg feeds the AXI crossbar access control vector. Therefore, any bus master capable of reaching the rom2_fuse AXI slave can overwrite the JTAG authentication key and all peripheral access control permissions for all masters.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged bus master can write arbitrary values to the JTAG key (disabling or substituting authentication) and to the access control bitmaps that govern every master's permission to reach every peripheral. This allows complete privilege escalation and bypass of the hardware-enforced access control policy, potentially enabling access to AES keys, PLIC, and all other protected peripherals.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full AXI crossbar and axi2mem implementations are not shown; it is theoretically possible (but not evidenced) that an upstream bus firewall blocks unprivileged writes to the rom2_fuse address range. No such firewall is visible in the provided source snippets.

finding.summary: Disabled peripheral stubs (SPI and Ethernet) unconditionally accept all AXI transactions and return 0xdeadbeef as read data with SLVERR, but with aw_ready/ar_ready/w_ready permanently asserted at 1, any master transaction is silently consumed without authentication or privilege check, and sensitive stub data (0xdeadbeef) is returned to any requester regardless of protection attributes (ar_prot, ar_lock) passed through verbatim.

evidence.description: When SPI is not included (InclSPI=0), the stub permanently asserts spi.aw_ready, spi.ar_ready, and spi.w_ready to 1'b1, accepts every transaction, and returns r_data='hdeadbeef with RESP_SLVERR. There is no guard checking ar_prot, ar_lock, or any privilege/security attribute before responding. The ar_lock signal from the master is passed through (line 345 in SNIP-0005) but never evaluated in the stub.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing permission/privilege guard on disabled peripheral stub: any AXI master can complete reads and writes to the SPI peripheral address range with no access control enforcement.

evidence.description: When Ethernet is not included (InclEthernet=0), identical stub logic permanently asserts ethernet.aw_ready, ethernet.ar_ready, and ethernet.w_ready, accepts all transactions, and returns r_data='hdeadbeef. No check on ar_prot, ar_lock, or any security attribute is present.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing permission/privilege guard on disabled peripheral stub: any AXI master can complete reads and writes to the Ethernet peripheral address range with no access control enforcement.

evidence.description: Lines 340-349 show ar_lock and ar_prot are passed verbatim from the master bus into ar_chan_i without any filtering or checking. The lock and protection attributes are forwarded but never used to gate access in any downstream stub or peripheral logic visible in the chain.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI protection/lock attributes (ar_lock, ar_prot, aw_lock, aw_prot) are wired through to the bus but not evaluated by stub responders, confirming the missing guard.

evidence.description: The testharness instantiates ariane_peripherals with InclUART=0, InclSPI=0, InclEthernet=0, meaning all three stub paths (with permanently-open ready signals and deadbeef readback) are active in the simulation environment, exposing the unguarded stubs to all connected AXI masters.
evidence.supports_claim: Test harness configuration activates all unguarded peripheral stubs simultaneously, making the missing access control exploitable in simulation by any master.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets show that when SPI and Ethernet peripherals are excluded (InclSPI=0, InclEthernet=0), stub logic is instantiated that permanently asserts all AXI ready signals and responds to every transaction without inspecting any security/privilege attributes (ar_prot, aw_prot, ar_lock, aw_lock). The ar_lock signal (the chain seed) is forwarded from the master into ar_chan_i (SNIP-0005, line 345) but is never checked before the stub accepts and responds to the transaction. The stub returns 0xdeadbeef on reads and RESP_SLVERR on both reads and writes, but the SLVERR response does not prevent the transaction from completing or block unauthorized access. The testharness instantiates peripherals with all three peripheral types disabled (SNIP-0006), activating all stub paths simultaneously.
finding.security_impact: Any AXI master connected to the peripheral crossbar can issue reads or writes to the SPI or Ethernet address ranges and receive responses without any privilege, lock, or protection-level check. In a testbench context this allows unrestricted access to peripheral register space. If similar stub logic is present in or migrated to a production context, it would represent a complete bypass of peripheral access controls.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain is rooted in a testbench file (tb/ariane_testharness.sv and tb/ariane_peripherals.sv). It is unclear whether these stubs exist only in simulation or are also synthesized. The ar_lock signal specifically is not shown being consumed by any access-control checker in the available snippets, but the absence of such a checker in the snippets does not conclusively prove none exists elsewhere in the design. Confidence is medium rather than high because the files are under tb/ (testbench) and the security impact in production depends on whether these modules are used outside simulation.

finding.summary: The debug module's dmstatus.authenticated is hardwired to 1'b1, meaning authentication is permanently bypassed. Any debugger connecting via JTAG or DMI is treated as fully authenticated without any credential check, enabling unrestricted debug access to all harts.

evidence.description: Line 171 unconditionally sets dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1 with the comment 'no authentication implemented'. This means the debug module always reports itself as authenticated, granting full debug access without any verification.
evidence.supports_claim: Authentication check is permanently bypassed in the debug module CSR logic.

evidence.description: The dm_csrs module exposes haltreq_o, resumereq_o, cmd_valid_o, and system bus access signals. These privileged debug operations become accessible to any connected debugger because the authenticated flag is hardwired to 1.
evidence.supports_claim: All debug control operations are available without authentication.

evidence.description: The testharness MUX routes either JTAG (SimJTAG) or DMI (SimDTM) debug requests to the debug module based on the jtag_enable plusarg, with no additional access control checks before forwarding to the debug module.
evidence.supports_claim: Multiple debug entry paths feed into the unauthenticated debug module.

evidence.description: The dmi_jtag instance in the testharness connects directly to the debug module. The jtag_key signal is wired in but there is no evidence in the snippets that it gates access before requests reach dm_csrs where authenticated is always 1.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG path reaches the debug module without enforced authentication.

evidence.description: dmcontrol fields including ndmreset (non-debug module reset), haltreq, and resumereq are writable through the debug interface. With authentication permanently bypassed, these safety-critical control signals can be manipulated by any debug client.
evidence.supports_claim: Safety-critical hart control signals are unprotected due to missing authentication.

evidence.description: ndmreset_o is driven directly from dmcontrol_q.ndmreset, and haltreq_o/resumereq_o are driven from dmcontrol_q fields. Any unauthenticated debugger can reset the entire SoC or halt/resume any hart.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthenticated debug access leads to full system compromise including reset and hart control.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0004 (line 171) contains the direct evidence: dmstatus.authenticated is hardwired to 1'b1 with an explicit comment stating 'no authentication implemented'. The RISC-V Debug Specification requires that when authentication is present, the authenticated bit must reflect the actual authentication state, and unauthenticated debuggers should be denied access to debug operations. Here, all debug operations including halt/resume requests, abstract commands, program buffer execution, and system bus access are fully exposed to any connecting debugger. The testharness provides two entry paths (JTAG via SimJTAG/dmi_jtag and direct DMI via SimDTM), both of which flow into the same unauthenticated debug module.
finding.security_impact: Any party with physical or logical access to the JTAG or DMI interface can fully control all harts: halt, resume, read/write registers and memory via abstract commands and system bus access, and trigger a full SoC reset via ndmreset. This constitutes complete privilege escalation bypassing all software security boundaries.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The testharness instantiates a jtag_key signal connected to dmi_jtag, which could potentially be part of an authentication mechanism implemented inside dmi_jtag. However, since dm_csrs.authenticated is hardwired to 1'b1 regardless, even if dmi_jtag checked the key, the dm_csrs module would still report authenticated=1 to any debugger. No snippet shows dm_csrs gating operations on an authentication input.

finding.summary: The debug module CSRs hardcode `dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1`, meaning no authentication is ever required to access debug functionality. Any agent with DMI access can unconditionally control halting, resuming, resetting harts, reading/writing memory via system bus access, and executing abstract commands.

evidence.description: Line 171 unconditionally assigns `dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1` with the comment 'no authentication implemented'. This permanently signals to the DTM that the debug module is authenticated, bypassing any authentication gate that would otherwise restrict debug access.
evidence.supports_claim: Authentication check is permanently bypassed; all debug operations are available to any DMI requester without credential validation.

evidence.description: The dm_csrs module accepts DMI requests (`dmi_req_valid_i`, `dmi_req_i`) from any source. `dmi_req_ready_o` is asserted whenever the response queue is not full, with no authentication gate on request acceptance.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface accepts requests unconditionally, relying on the authentication field to gate access, but that field is hardwired to authenticated.

evidence.description: Line 151 shows `dmi_req_ready_o = ~resp_queue_full`, and line 152 shows `resp_queue_push = dmi_req_valid_i & dmi_req_ready_o`. There is no authentication check in the handshake path; requests are accepted purely based on queue availability.
evidence.supports_claim: No authentication guard exists in the request acceptance path.

evidence.description: The dm_csrs module drives `haltreq_o`, `resumereq_o`, `ndmreset_o`, `cmd_valid_o`, `sbaddress_write_valid_o`, and `sbdata_write_valid_o` — all sensitive debug control outputs — derived from unauthenticated DMI writes.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive hart control and system bus access operations are reachable without authentication.

evidence.description: The testharness declares `debug_req_valid` and `debug_req_ready` as plain logic signals connected to the debug subsystem, confirming the debug request path is present and exercisable in the design.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug request infrastructure is wired up and active, making the unauthenticated access practically exploitable.

finding.reasoning_summary: The RISC-V Debug Specification v0.13 defines `dmstatus.authenticated` as a bit that, when 0, prevents the debugger from performing any operations other than reading `dmstatus` and writing `dmcontrol.dmactive`. In this implementation, `dmstatus.authenticated` is hardwired to 1 with an explicit comment acknowledging that authentication is not implemented. As a result, any agent that can issue DMI transactions — including an attacker with JTAG physical access — immediately has full debug authority: halting/resuming arbitrary harts, triggering non-debug module reset (`ndmreset`), executing abstract commands, and performing 64-bit system bus reads/writes to arbitrary addresses.
finding.security_impact: Complete bypass of debug authentication. An unauthenticated attacker with JTAG or DMI access can halt all CPU cores, read and write arbitrary physical memory via the system bus access interface, inject code via the program buffer, and assert a non-debug module reset — effectively achieving full system compromise.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is theoretically possible that an outer wrapper enforces authentication before granting DMI access, but no such wrapper is present in the provided source snippets. The comment in the source code ('no authentication implemented') directly confirms the intent.

finding.summary: The debug module's dmstatus.authenticated is hardwired to 1'b1, meaning authentication is permanently bypassed. Any debugger connecting over DMI/JTAG is unconditionally granted authenticated access to the debug module without any credential check.

evidence.description: Line 171 sets dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1 unconditionally with the comment 'no authentication implemented'. This permanently asserts the authenticated bit in the RISC-V debug spec's dmstatus register, bypassing any authentication requirement.
evidence.supports_claim: Authentication is hardwired to always-pass, removing any access control gate on the debug module.

evidence.description: The debug request MUX in the testharness routes either JTAG or DMI requests directly to the debug module based on jtag_enable[0], with no additional privilege or authentication check before forwarding debug requests.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug access reaches the debug module from simulation/external interfaces without any authentication step.

evidence.description: The dm_csrs module interface includes a testmode_i input but no authentication input or output port. There is no mechanism for an external authentication handshake to gate access.
evidence.supports_claim: The module design provides no hook for external authentication, confirming the bypass is architectural, not merely a configuration choice.

evidence.description: The debug module exposes haltreq_o, resumereq_o, ndmreset_o, cmd_valid_o, and progbuf_o — powerful debug controls over all harts — all reachable without authentication.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthenticated access enables halting, resetting, and executing arbitrary abstract commands on all harts.

evidence.description: The dmi_jtag instance receives a jtag_key input but there is no corresponding check in dm_csrs that gates operations on authentication state, since authenticated is hardwired to 1.
evidence.supports_claim: Even if a key mechanism exists in dmi_jtag, the debug CSR layer ignores it and grants full access unconditionally.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0004 provides direct source-code evidence that dmstatus.authenticated is permanently set to 1'b1 with an explicit comment acknowledging the omission. The RISC-V Debug Specification requires that debug operations be gated on the authenticated bit; when it is always 1, no authentication challenge is ever issued. Combined with the open DMI/JTAG access path shown in SNIP-0009 and the powerful debug resources exposed in SNIP-0007, any party with physical or simulated JTAG/DMI access can fully control all processor harts without any credential verification.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG or DMI interface access can halt, resume, reset, and execute arbitrary abstract commands (including arbitrary memory/register read-write via progbuf) on all processor harts with no authentication barrier. This enables complete system compromise including extraction of secrets and arbitrary code execution at machine privilege level.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The jtag_key signal passed to dmi_jtag (SNIP-0010) could theoretically implement a transport-layer key check, but its implementation is not in the provided snippets. However, since dm_csrs unconditionally asserts authenticated regardless of any upstream check, even a working key in dmi_jtag would not close the vulnerability at the CSR policy layer.

finding.summary: The debug module's DMI response always returns DTM_SUCCESS regardless of the actual operation outcome, and the dmstatus register unconditionally asserts 'authenticated = 1' with no authentication mechanism implemented, exposing full debug access without any privilege or authentication check.

evidence.description: Line 149 unconditionally assigns dmi_resp_o.resp = dm::DTM_SUCCESS, meaning every debug module interface response always reports success regardless of whether the operation was valid, authorized, or completed correctly.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug response channel never signals an error or unauthorized status back to the JTAG host, removing a key feedback mechanism for access control enforcement.

evidence.description: Line 171 unconditionally sets dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1 with the comment 'no authentication implemented', meaning any JTAG host querying dmstatus will always see the debug module as authenticated and fully accessible.
evidence.supports_claim: Without an authentication gate, any attacker with physical or logical JTAG access obtains full debug module privileges (halt, resume, memory read/write via system bus access) without any credential check.

evidence.description: The dm_csrs module exposes all debug CSR operations (halt, resume, abstract commands, program buffer, system bus access) through the DMI interface. The absence of any authentication check means all these capabilities are always available.
evidence.supports_claim: The protected resource (CPU halt/resume control, arbitrary memory access via SBA) is reachable through the debug interface with no permission barrier.

evidence.description: The dmi_jtag module accepts a jtag_key input (line 24) suggesting a key-based authentication was planned but the dm_csrs module never checks any such key when setting authenticated=1.
evidence.supports_claim: A jtag_key port exists in the interface definition but authenticated is hardwired to 1 in dm_csrs, so the key is never used as an actual access gate.

evidence.description: In ariane_testharness, the dmi_jtag instance is wired with a jtag_key signal, but since dm_csrs.dmstatus.authenticated is always 1, the key provides no actual authentication enforcement.
evidence.supports_claim: The testharness wires jtag_key to the JTAG module but the downstream dm_csrs never consults it, confirming the bypass.

evidence.description: Lines 363-407 show full read/write access to system bus address and data registers (SBAddress0/1, SBData0/1) which allow arbitrary physical memory access, all reachable without authentication.
evidence.supports_claim: Arbitrary memory read/write via the system bus access path is accessible to any JTAG user because authenticated is always 1 and responses always return SUCCESS.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets provide direct evidence that: (1) dm_csrs unconditionally sets dmi_resp_o.resp = dm::DTM_SUCCESS on every response (SNIP-0006 line 149), and (2) dm_csrs unconditionally sets dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1 with an explicit comment that no authentication is implemented (SNIP-0007 line 171). Although a jtag_key port exists in dmi_jtag (SNIP-0014), it is never used to gate access in dm_csrs. The combination means any entity with JTAG connectivity can halt/resume CPUs, read/write arbitrary memory through the system bus access module, and inject abstract commands — all without any credential check. The always-SUCCESS response also prevents the host from detecting access violations.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access (physical or, in FPGA/testbench contexts, logical) can unconditionally halt processor cores, read and write arbitrary physical memory via the system bus access mechanism, inject arbitrary abstract commands, and modify program buffer contents. The hardwired authenticated=1 and always-DTM_SUCCESS response eliminate both the authentication barrier and error reporting, providing complete system compromise capability through the debug interface.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The jtag_key port in dmi_jtag could potentially implement key-checking logic within dmi_jtag itself before forwarding requests; the dmi_jtag internal logic beyond the port declaration is not fully shown in the provided snippets. However, dm_csrs.dmstatus.authenticated is hardwired to 1 regardless, which is the authoritative authentication signal per the RISC-V debug specification, so even if dmi_jtag had some key check it would not change the authenticated status visible to the host.

finding.summary: The dmi_jtag module accepts a jtag_key input that is wired in the testharness but there is no evidence of key validation logic guarding DMI access; simultaneously, the debug interface mux in ariane_testharness selects between JTAG and software DTM paths using only a simulation plusarg (jtag_rbb_enable), with no hardware privilege or authentication check, allowing unrestricted debug module access in any mode.

evidence.description: dmi_jtag module port list shows a jtag_key input (line 24) and a umode_o output (line 41) suggesting a key-based access control mechanism exists, but the module interface alone provides no evidence of enforcement logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The jtag_key port implies an intended authentication mechanism whose enforcement is not visible in the snippets provided.

evidence.description: In ariane_testharness, jtag_enable is set from simulation plusarg jtag_rbb_enable (line 108). The debug IF mux (lines 112-117) selects JTAG or DTM paths purely on this simulation-time integer. jtag_key is wired (line 139) but no guard checks its value before passing debug_req_valid and debug_req to dm_top.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug access path selection relies on an unguarded simulation plusarg with no hardware permission check.

evidence.description: dm_csrs unconditionally sets dmi_resp_valid_o = ~resp_queue_empty (line 150) and dmi_req_ready_o = ~resp_queue_full (line 151). There is no check on any authentication signal or privilege level before accepting or responding to DMI requests.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug CSR module accepts all incoming DMI transactions without any permission or authentication guard.

evidence.description: resp_queue_pop = dmi_resp_ready_i & ~resp_queue_empty (line 475) — the only condition for popping debug responses is that the consumer signals ready and the queue is non-empty. No privilege, lock, or authentication state gates this path.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug response data is readable by any consumer that asserts dmi_resp_ready_i without any access-control check.

evidence.description: dm_top is instantiated (lines 185-194) with debug_req_valid and debug_req driven directly from the mux output, and debug_resp_ready connected without any interposed permission check.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug module top-level receives unauthenticated DMI requests and returns responses to any ready consumer.

evidence.description: jtag_key is passed to i_dmi_jtag (line 139) but its value is never checked in any shown guard logic before debug_req_valid or debug_req reach dm_top. The jtag_enable mux at line 112-114 ignores jtag_key entirely.
evidence.supports_claim: The jtag_key authentication input is bypassed: the mux controlling dm_top access does not condition on jtag_key validity.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug interface chain connects dmi_jtag (with a jtag_key input) through a mux controlled only by a simulation plusarg into dm_top/dm_csrs. The dm_csrs module accepts all DMI requests unconditionally (no auth/privilege check on dmi_req_ready_o or dmi_resp_valid_o). The jtag_key field present in the dmi_jtag port is wired in the testharness but no snippet shows it being validated before debug access is granted. This means any agent asserting dmi_req_valid can read/write debug CSRs including halt requests, program buffer, and system bus access registers without authentication.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug transport (JTAG or simulation DTM) can issue arbitrary DMI transactions to halt harts, inject code via the program buffer, perform system bus reads/writes to any physical address, and read sensitive debug state — all without passing any authentication or privilege check. The jtag_key mechanism appears structurally present but is not enforced in the access path shown.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal implementation of dmi_jtag (specifically whether jtag_key is validated internally before asserting dmi_req_valid_o) is not fully shown in the snippets. If dmi_jtag internally gates dmi_req_valid_o on jtag_key comparison, the bypass via jtag_enable mux would still exist but the JTAG path itself might be guarded. The simulation-only nature of ariane_testharness also limits real-silicon impact assessment, though ariane_xilinx.sv shows the same dm_top instantiation pattern in an FPGA target without key checks visible.

finding.summary: The dmi_jtag module exposes a jtag_key input port in its interface but the source snippets show no guard logic that validates this key before allowing JTAG debug access to the debug module. The debug interface MUX in ariane_testharness routes JTAG or DMI requests directly to the debug module (dm_top) based solely on the jtag_enable simulation plusarg, with no authentication or key-validation check visible in the RTL chain.

evidence.description: The dmi_jtag module declares a jtag_key input port (line 24: 'input logic [31:0] jtag_key') alongside full debug request/response signals. The presence of this key port implies an authentication mechanism, but no guard logic consuming this key is present anywhere in the supplied source snippets.
evidence.supports_claim: A key-based authentication port exists in the interface but is never checked in the observable RTL, meaning the key is accepted as input but never used to gate debug access.

evidence.description: In ariane_testharness, jtag_key is passed to i_dmi_jtag (line 139: '.jtag_key ( jtag_key )'), but jtag_key itself is declared as an uninitialized logic signal (SNIP-0014, line 65) with no assignment shown, meaning it is driven to an unknown/zero value and no check on its value gates the debug interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The jtag_key value passed to the JTAG debug interface is never validated or initialized, undermining any intended key-based access control.

evidence.description: The jtag_key signal is declared at line 65 ('logic [31:0] jtag_key') with no assignment in the testharness, meaning it floats or defaults to zero. No initialization, no comparison, and no conditional gating based on jtag_key is present.
evidence.supports_claim: The key variable is never driven to a meaningful value, and there is no downstream check, so the authentication mechanism is effectively absent.

evidence.description: The debug interface MUX (lines 112-117) routes debug requests to dm_top based only on jtag_enable[0] (a simulation plusarg), with no check on jtag_key or any other authentication signal. The MUX is purely a functional selector between JTAG and DMI paths.
evidence.supports_claim: No authentication guard is present between the JTAG interface and the debug module; access is gated only by a simulation configuration flag, not a security check.

evidence.description: dm_csrs unconditionally exposes dmi_resp_valid_o (line 150: 'assign dmi_resp_valid_o = ~resp_queue_empty') and accepts all incoming dmi_req_valid_i requests without any permission or key validation, meaning any caller that gets past the interface MUX has full debug CSR access.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag module interface includes a jtag_key port suggesting an intended authentication mechanism. However, in both the testharness (ariane_testharness.sv) and the FPGA target (ariane_xilinx.sv), no code initializes or validates this key. The debug interface MUX routes requests to dm_top based only on a simulation plusarg (jtag_enable). The dm_top and dm_csrs modules apply no authentication of their own. The result is that the JTAG debug interface provides unrestricted access to the processor debug module with no key or privilege check enforced anywhere in the RTL chain.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or simulated JTAG access can issue arbitrary debug module interface (DMI) requests—halting harts, reading/writing memory and registers, injecting program buffer code, and triggering system resets—without providing any valid credential. The jtag_key mechanism is effectively dead code.

finding.summary: The debug module's system bus access (SBA) master AXI response port is permanently tied to zero in serpent_peripherals, disabling SBA functionality silently without error signaling, while the debug module still drives AXI master requests that will receive no real bus responses.

evidence.description: Line 196 statically assigns dm_axi_m_resp = '0, tying off all AXI response channels (RVALID, BVALID, RDATA, etc.) to zero. This is explicitly commented as 'tie off system bus accesses (not supported yet)'. The debug module's SBA master will never receive valid AXI responses, meaning any system bus access initiated via the debug module will hang or silently fail without proper error reporting.
evidence.supports_claim: The system bus access port of the debug module receives no real responses; any SBA operation will stall waiting for a valid AXI response that never arrives, creating a denial-of-service or silent failure condition for debug-initiated memory accesses.

evidence.description: dm_top declares axi_m_resp_i as an input for system bus access responses. The debug module uses this port to receive results of system bus reads/writes initiated by a debugger. The SBA mechanism allows a debugger to read/write arbitrary physical memory addresses on the system bus.
evidence.supports_claim: The SBA interface of the debug module is a privileged path that can access arbitrary system memory; tying its response to zero rather than properly gating or error-responding leaves the SBA state machine in an undefined/hung state.

evidence.description: serpent_peripherals instantiates dm_top and connects dm_axi_m_resp to the debug module's axi_m_resp_i port. The tied-off value flows directly into the debug module's SBA response input.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms the signal path from the tie-off assignment into the active dm_top instance used in the OpenPiton integration.

evidence.description: In ariane_xilinx, dm_axi_m_resp is declared but the snippet does not show it being assigned to zero; however, the FPGA integration also instantiates dm_top with this port, and the absence of a proper interconnect or tie-off assignment needs verification.
evidence.supports_claim: Indicates the same pattern exists in the FPGA target, where dm_axi_m_resp may be left undriven or improperly handled.

finding.reasoning_summary: In serpent_peripherals (OpenPiton integration), the AXI master response port of the debug module (used for system bus accesses) is permanently tied to all-zeros via a continuous assignment. This is explicitly acknowledged in the comment as unsupported. The consequence is that any RISC-V debug system bus access (SBA) operation will issue AXI requests but never receive a valid response (RVALID or BVALID will never assert), causing the SBA state machine inside dm_top to stall indefinitely. This represents a missing proper error/tie-off response that could cause the debug subsystem to hang, potentially blocking other debug operations or causing watchdog/timeout issues. Furthermore, if the SBA feature is inadvertently enabled by a debugger, it could lock up the debug module entirely, constituting a denial-of-service against the debug infrastructure.
finding.security_impact: A debugger that enables system bus access will cause the debug module's SBA state machine to stall waiting for AXI responses that never arrive (RVALID/BVALID permanently 0). This can lock the entire debug module, preventing further debug operations including halting or resuming harts. In a production system where this integration is used, this represents a debug infrastructure availability issue and potential security-relevant behavior if an attacker can trigger SBA operations to freeze the debug module.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed whether the ariane_xilinx FPGA integration has a similar or different tie-off for dm_axi_m_resp (the snippet shows declaration but not the full port connection list). The testharness variant also lacks explicit evidence of how dm_axi_m_resp is driven. The analysis is high-confidence for the serpent_peripherals case based on direct source evidence in SNIP-0003.

finding.summary: The debug module (dm_top) AXI slave port receives requests with AXI protection field (prot) hardwired to zero, meaning no privilege, security, or instruction/data access type information is conveyed to the debug module, bypassing any access-control differentiation based on AXI AxPROT.

evidence.description: In serpent_peripherals.sv lines 205 and 216, dm_axi_s_req.aw.prot and dm_axi_s_req.ar.prot are both tied to '0 via continuous assign. This forces the AXI protection attribute to unprivileged, non-secure, data access for all debug module slave requests, regardless of the actual initiator's privilege or security context.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI AxPROT bits are hardwired to zero for all accesses to the debug module slave port, eliminating any access-control enforcement that would otherwise rely on privilege or security level signaling through the AXI bus.

evidence.description: dm_top is the RISC-V debug module (AXI slave), accepting axi_s_req_i. It controls hart halting, resuming, program buffer execution, and system bus access — all highly privileged debug operations. The prot field of incoming AXI requests could be used to restrict access to trusted/privileged initiators only.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug module is a sensitive resource; all accesses to it arrive with prot=0, meaning the module cannot distinguish privileged from unprivileged or secure from non-secure requestors.

evidence.description: serpent_peripherals instantiates i_dm_top and connects dm_axi_s_req directly to axi_s_req_i without interposing any privilege or security guard.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug module slave port is directly wired from the constructed dm_axi_s_req signal, whose protection fields are zeroed out as shown in SNIP-0003.

evidence.description: ariane_xilinx also instantiates dm_top with dm_axi_s_req connected to axi_s_req_i, sharing the same pattern of no privilege guard on the debug slave AXI path.
evidence.supports_claim: The FPGA integration also routes debug module slave requests without enforcing AXI protection attributes.

evidence.description: ariane_testharness also instantiates i_dm_top with dm_axi_s_req connected to the debug module's slave AXI port, confirming the pattern is present across testbench, FPGA, and OpenPiton integration contexts.
evidence.supports_claim: The missing protection attribute on debug module slave accesses is a systemic pattern across all integration contexts, not an isolated oversight.

finding.reasoning_summary: Across all three integration contexts (testbench, FPGA, OpenPiton), the debug module's AXI slave port (axi_s_req_i) receives requests where aw.prot and ar.prot are tied to zero. In serpent_peripherals.sv this is explicit via continuous assignments (lines 205, 216). The AXI ARPROT/AWPROT encoding carries privilege level (bit 0), security level (bit 1), and instruction/data (bit 2). Hardwiring these to zero means: (1) any receiver logic that gates access on privilege level sees all requests as unprivileged, (2) any security domain separation enforced via AXI AxPROT is bypassed, and (3) the debug module — which has full halt/resume/memory access capability over CPU harts — can be accessed without the bus fabric being able to distinguish a trusted initiator from an untrusted one based on protection attributes.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or non-secure AXI master that can reach the debug module's slave address space will have its requests treated identically to a privileged secure master, since all requests arrive with prot=0. This could allow unauthorized debug access to CPU harts, enabling halt, register read/write, program buffer execution, and arbitrary memory access via the system bus interface of the debug module.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether dm_top internally enforces any access control based on the AXI prot field is not fully shown in the provided snippets (only lines 14-93 of dm_top.sv are visible). If dm_top does not use prot internally and relies on the fabric to gate access, the impact depends on fabric-level filtering. The testharness and FPGA contexts do not show explicit prot zeroing (only serpent_peripherals does via explicit assigns), so those may rely on default initialization. Additionally, whether any upstream AXI crossbar enforces AxPROT-based filtering before reaching dm_top is not visible in this chain.

While this is a testharness/simulation context, the dm_top module is the real production debug module, and the lack of any auth guard means the DMI simulation path has unrestricted access to full debug operations on the processor.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unauthorized simulation environment can drive arbitrary DMI requests to the debug module (dm_top) via the SimDTM DPI interface, enabling full debug control over the processor including halting, memory access, and register modification without any authentication or privilege check.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: This is a testharness (tb/) file, so findings may be intentional for simulation use.
