finding.reasoning_summary: The RISC-V debug specification requires that when authentication is implemented, the authenticated bit in dmstatus must reflect the actual authentication state and debug operations must be blocked until authentication succeeds. Here, dmstatus.authenticated is hardwired to 1'b1 (SNIP-0007 line 171, comment explicitly states 'no authentication implemented'), while the dmi_jtag module accepts a jtag_key input (SNIP-0011) suggesting authentication was intended. The key is passed in from the testharness (SNIP-0014 line 139) but is never used to gate the dmi_req_valid_o output or any other debug access signal. As a result, any JTAG connection can immediately issue arbitrary DMI transactions to the debug module without any credential verification, across all three integration targets (testharness, FPGA, OpenPiton).
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical access to the JTAG interface can halt processor cores, read and write arbitrary memory via the system bus access module, inject code via the program buffer, and trigger a full system reset—all without any authentication. This completely undermines any software-level security boundary.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal implementation of dmi_jtag where jtag_key might be consumed is not fully shown in the snippets. It is possible that key checking occurs inside dmi_jtag and gates an internal signal before dmi_req_valid_o is asserted, but the port declaration (SNIP-0011) and the unconditional authenticated=1 in dm_csrs (SNIP-0007) together strongly indicate no effective authentication is enforced end-to-end.

finding.security_impact: An attacker or unauthorized simulation agent can issue arbitrary debug module interface (DMI) requests to halt, resume, or single-step processor harts, read/write general-purpose registers, execute abstract commands, and perform system bus accesses to arbitrary memory addresses — all without any authentication or privilege verification.

finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG physical access (or simulation control) can issue arbitrary DMI transactions to the debug module, gaining the ability to halt/resume harts, execute abstract commands, access the program buffer, and perform system bus reads/writes — all without presenting a valid jtag_key. However, no evidence of such internal gating is visible, and no auth_valid or key_ok output port exists on dmi_jtag to carry that result outward. The testharness (tb/ariane_testharness.sv) connects a jtag_key port, implying the dmi_jtag module supports an authentication mechanism. Neither the FPGA production top-level (ariane_xilinx.sv) nor the OpenPiton integration (serpent_peripherals.sv) connects this port, meaning the authentication mechanism is absent in all production builds. finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG pad access on production hardware can issue arbitrary DMI (Debug Module Interface) requests, read/write any memory or register, halt the processor, and force execution at machine privilege level. No authentication or lifecycle guard prevents this access in the production RTL.

finding.summary: The JTAG debug access control in dmi_jtag implements a password check (pass_chk) to gate read access via DTM_READ, but the password check mechanism is fundamentally broken: pass_chk is a combinational signal that is never latched/persisted across clock cycles, and the DTM_PASS handler unconditionally forces state_d back to Idle after writing pass_chk, meaning any subsequent DTM_READ in a new JTAG transaction will find pass_chk=0 (its default) and be blocked — yet the write path (DTM_WRITE) has no password guard at all and is always permitted.

evidence.description: pass_chk is declared as a plain logic signal (line 79) with no register backing. In the always_comb block it defaults to its previous combinational value only within the same evaluation; it is never assigned in the always_ff block (lines 212-226), so it does not survive clock edges.
evidence.supports_claim: The password gate (pass_chk) is not a registered/persistent permission bit; it resets to 0 on every combinational re-evaluation outside the DTM_PASS operation.

evidence.description: The DTM_PASS handler (lines 114-120) sets pass_chk=1 and then immediately overwrites state_d=Idle in the same combinational block. Because pass_chk is not latched into a flip-flop, any future DTM_READ transaction starts with pass_chk=0, making the guard permanently ineffective for cross-transaction enforcement.
evidence.supports_claim: The password check cannot persist across JTAG transactions because pass_chk is purely combinational and has no flip-flop storage.

evidence.description: The DTM_WRITE path (line 112: 'else if (dm::dtm_op_t'(dmi.op) == dm::DTM_WRITE)') transitions directly to the Write state with no check on pass_chk. An attacker can issue write operations to the debug module interface without ever providing the password.
evidence.supports_claim: Write access to the debug module is completely unguarded by the password mechanism, creating an asymmetric and incomplete access control implementation.

evidence.description: The always_ff block (lines 212-226) resets and updates dr_q, state_q, address_q, data_q, error_q — but pass_chk is absent from both the reset clause and the normal update clause, confirming it has no registered state.
evidence.supports_claim: pass_chk is never stored in a flip-flop, so it cannot serve as a durable permission gate.

evidence.description: umode_o (line 178-182) is driven high only when pass_chk==1, which — given the broken persistence — will only be asserted transiently within the single combinational evaluation of the DTM_PASS operation and never durably, defeating the intended privilege escalation gating to machine mode.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege output umode_o that sets the processor to machine mode is incorrectly gated on a non-persistent signal, allowing either spurious assertion or permanent de-assertion.

evidence.description: The testbench instantiation of dmi_jtag (lines 135-144) connects jtag_TRSTn directly to trst_ni with no additional access control, providing the JTAG reset entry point that flows into the security-sensitive debug logic.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG TRSTn from the testbench directly resets the debug state machine, which relies on the broken pass_chk mechanism.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag module attempts to implement a password-based access control where a DTM_PASS operation sets pass_chk=1 to unlock subsequent DTM_READ operations. However, pass_chk is declared as combinational logic with no flip-flop backing — it is not present in the always_ff reset or update block. This means the permission bit cannot persist across clock edges or JTAG transactions. Additionally, the DTM_WRITE path bypasses pass_chk entirely, and umode_o (the machine-mode privilege output) is gated on the same broken signal. The net effect is that the access control mechanism provides no real protection: writes are always allowed, reads are always blocked (since pass_chk never persists), and machine-mode privilege is never durably granted.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can issue DTM_WRITE operations to the debug module interface without any password, potentially reading/writing arbitrary system registers and memory through the debug module. The intended privilege escalation control (umode_o) and read protection are both non-functional due to the combinational-only pass_chk signal.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full source of the password initialization from ROM2 (referenced at line 228 comment) and the jtag_key input usage are not fully shown in the provided snippets, so the complete intended password flow cannot be verified. However, the combinational nature of pass_chk and its absence from the sequential block is clearly evidenced in SNIP-0005.

finding.summary: The jtag_enable mux control signal is derived from a plusargs simulation argument without any hardware authentication or privilege check, and the dmi_jtag module exposes a jtag_key input port that is wired in the testharness but there is no evidence in dm_csrs that authentication is enforced — dmstatus.authenticated is hardcoded to 1'b1, meaning the debug module always reports itself as authenticated regardless of any key or credential.

evidence.description: jtag_enable is set from a simulation plusarg ($value$plusargs("jtag_rbb_enable=%b", jtag_enable)) with a default of 0. It is used as a mux select for the debug interface with no hardware authentication gate. The jtag_key is passed to dmi_jtag but the mux itself is purely controlled by a simulation plusarg.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface mux selector is a simulation-time value with no RTL-enforced permission or privilege check before granting debug access.

evidence.description: In dm_csrs, dmstatus.authenticated is unconditionally assigned 1'b1 at line 171: '// no authentication implemented' followed by 'dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1;'. This means the debug module always reports the debugger as authenticated to any DMI requester, bypassing any authentication mechanism.
evidence.supports_claim: Authentication is permanently hardcoded to pass, so even if jtag_key were checked in dmi_jtag, the dm_csrs module itself never enforces an authenticated state — it unconditionally grants authenticated status.

evidence.description: dmi_jtag module interface declares a jtag_key input port (line 24), suggesting a key-based authentication mechanism was intended, but the dm_csrs module's authenticated bit is hardcoded to 1 regardless.
evidence.supports_claim: A key port exists implying authentication intent, but the downstream permission state is never conditioned on it.

evidence.description: jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg are declared as signals in ariane_testharness but there is no guard logic shown that conditions debug access on their values before forwarding requests to dm_top.
evidence.supports_claim: Signals suggesting key-based access control exist but are not shown to gate the debug request path.

evidence.description: dm_top instantiates dm_csrs and passes dmi_req_valid_i and dmi_req_i directly without any interposing authentication check. The debug module CSRs (halt/resume control, abstract commands, program buffer) are fully accessible once DMI requests arrive.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug CSR register bank including hart halt/resume and program buffer is accessible with no authentication guard at the dm_top or dm_csrs boundary.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain shows a debug interface where: (1) jtag_enable is controlled by a simulation plusarg with no RTL-enforced authentication; (2) in dm_csrs, dmstatus.authenticated is hardcoded to 1'b1 with an explicit comment 'no authentication implemented'; (3) dmi_jtag has a jtag_key port suggesting authentication was planned but it is never checked against the permission state in dm_csrs; (4) DMI requests flow from dmi_jtag through dm_top into dm_csrs without any authentication gate. The combination of an always-authenticated debug module and an unguarded mux means any entity that can assert dmi_req_valid can fully control debug operations.
finding.security_impact: Any entity with access to the DMI interface (JTAG or simulated DTM) can halt, resume, and execute arbitrary code on all harts via the debug abstract command interface, read/write all memory via the system bus access interface, and control the non-debug-module reset (ndmreset), all without any authentication check being enforced in hardware.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full implementation of dmi_jtag's jtag_key usage is not shown in the snippets — it is possible the key check happens inside dmi_jtag and gates dmi_req_valid_o, but the dm_csrs authenticated=1 hardcoding remains a concrete unconditional bypass regardless. The testharness is a simulation file (tb/), but ariane_xilinx.sv and serpent_peripherals.sv show the same pattern in production FPGA targets with no authentication guard visible.

finding.summary: The JTAG authentication password (`pass`) is loaded from `jtag_key` only on reset (active-low rst_ni de-asserted), not during normal operation. More critically, the `pass_chk` flag that gates JTAG read access is a combinational register that is never reset or cleared — once set to 1'b1, it persists for the entire simulation/operation without any revocation mechanism. Additionally, the password comparison occurs in purely combinational logic with `pass_chk` being assigned inside an `always_comb` block but relying on the registered `pass` value that is itself only initialized on reset, allowing authentication bypass scenarios.

evidence.description: The `pass` register is loaded from `jtag_key` only inside `always_ff @ (posedge clk_i)` with condition `if (~rst_ni)`. This means the password is only captured at reset time and never updated after reset, and crucially, if rst_ni never goes low after startup, `pass` retains its uninitialized/default value of 0 — making the password trivially 0.
evidence.supports_claim: Password register initialized only on reset; if reset does not occur, pass=0 allowing trivial bypass with a zero-valued DTM_PASS operation.

evidence.description: Lines 110-120: The DTM_READ operation is gated by `pass_chk == 1'b1`, and pass_chk is set to 1'b1 when `data_d == pass` during a DTM_PASS operation. However, `pass_chk` is declared as a plain `logic` variable (line 79) and assigned inside `always_comb`. It is never explicitly reset to 0 in the sequential always_ff block, making it retain its last combinational value across clock cycles with no architectural reset or revocation path. Furthermore, the password check `data_d == pass` compares against `pass` which defaults to 0 unless reset fires.
evidence.supports_claim: pass_chk is set combinatorially and never cleared via flip-flop reset, meaning once authentication succeeds it cannot be revoked; also the check can be trivially bypassed if pass=0.

evidence.description: The rom2 module stores the JTAG key as a hardcoded constant in RTL (`192'h2b7e1516_28aed2a6_abf71588_09cf4f3c_2b7e1516_28aed2a6` at index 1). The key is directly readable from RTL source and exposed as `secure_reg` output signal with no access protection on the output port.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG key is a hardcoded constant in RTL and exposed as a plain output port, making the secret trivially discoverable by reading the source or the output signal.

evidence.description: Line 215: `assign jtag_key = key_reg_out[1][31:0]` — only the lower 32 bits of the 192-bit key entry are used as the JTAG password. The `secure_reg` / `key_reg_out` array is directly wired out without any protection or access control logic gating this path.
evidence.supports_claim: The JTAG authentication key is propagated as a plain combinational wire from rom2 output to dmi_jtag with no intermediary protection.

evidence.description: Lines 114-120: The DTM_PASS handler sets `pass_chk = 1'b1` inside the `always_comb` block when `data_d == pass`, then immediately sets `state_d = Idle`. Since `pass_chk` is not a registered output (no corresponding flip-flop captures it), the `always_comb` assignment of pass_chk=1 during DTM_PASS will only hold for one combinational evaluation cycle. However, `pass_chk` is READ at line 110 (`if (... && (pass_chk == 1'b1))`) within the same `always_comb` block. Because `pass_chk` is listed as a `logic` without a register, this creates a race/ordering dependency in the combinational block where pass_chk set within the DTM_PASS branch of the case statement does not persist to gatekeep subsequent DMI_READ operations across clock cycles — the authentication state is not durably stored.
evidence.supports_claim: Authentication state (pass_chk) is set combinatorially within always_comb but never latched into a flip-flop, so its value does not persist across clock cycles, effectively making the JTAG read permission gate non-functional as a security control.

evidence.description: The testharness directly wires `jtag_key` from `ariane_peripherals` to `dmi_jtag` without any additional authorization layer, and the dmi_jtag module receives the key as a plain input port. The jtag_key signal is declared as a plain `logic [31:0]` in the testharness (SNIP-0016, line 65) with no protection.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG key is passed as an unprotected plain signal through the testharness into the debug module, enabling key observation or manipulation in simulation.

finding.reasoning_summary: Multiple security violations exist in the JTAG authentication chain. First, the password register `pass` is only loaded during reset from `jtag_key`, so if reset is not asserted, `pass` remains 0 — trivially bypassable. Second, `pass_chk` is assigned inside `always_comb` but not captured in any `always_ff` register, meaning the authentication flag does not durably persist across clock cycles, rendering the DTM_READ gate ineffective. Third, the JTAG key itself is a hardcoded constant in the RTL source (rom2.sv) and is exposed as a plain combinational output without any access protection. The entire authentication mechanism can be bypassed by either sending a zero-value DTM_PASS request (if reset never fires) or by exploiting the non-persistent combinational nature of pass_chk.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can bypass the authentication gate to issue arbitrary debug read/write operations to the debug module (dm_top), enabling full control over the processor including memory reads/writes, register access, and halt/resume of all harts. The hardcoded key in RTL also means any party with source access knows the secret, nullifying the authentication entirely.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact synthesis behavior of `pass_chk` in always_comb without a corresponding flip-flop depends on tool interpretation — some tools may infer a latch. The full always_ff block for pass_chk (if any) beyond line 231 is not shown. However, SNIP-0010 clearly shows only `pass` is registered on reset with no pass_chk registration, supporting the finding.

finding.summary: The dmi_jtag module declares a jtag_key input port intended as a JTAG authentication key, but the testharness instantiation passes jtag_key without any verification logic visible in the chain, and the debug interface MUX that routes JTAG requests to the debug module (dm_top) is controlled solely by a simulation plusarg (jtag_enable) with no hardware authentication guard, allowing unrestricted debug access.

evidence.description: dmi_jtag module interface declares a jtag_key input port (line 24) suggesting an authentication mechanism, but also declares umode_o (line 41) which sets processor to machine mode. The key input exists at the interface boundary but no guard logic consuming it is visible in the chain.
evidence.supports_claim: A JTAG key parameter exists in the interface but its enforcement is absent from the provided evidence chain.

evidence.description: The debug interface MUX at lines 112-117 routes jtag_req_valid, jtag_resp_ready, jtag_dmi_req to the debug module based solely on jtag_enable[0], a simulation plusarg defaulting to 0. The jtag_key passed at line 139 is forwarded to i_dmi_jtag but no conditional guard checks key validity before enabling debug access. jtag_resp_valid is assigned debug_resp_valid unconditionally when jtag_enable[0] is set.
evidence.supports_claim: No authentication or privilege check guards the routing of JTAG debug requests to dm_top; only a simulation plusarg controls the mux.

evidence.description: dm_csrs assigns dmi_resp_valid_o = ~resp_queue_empty and dmi_resp_o.resp = DTM_SUCCESS unconditionally (lines 149-150). Any request that enters the queue receives a success response with no authentication check inside dm_csrs.

evidence.description: jtag_key is declared as a 32-bit signal (line 65) and access_ctrl_reg as a 2x48-bit register (line 66) suggesting intended access control, but neither is used in the MUX guard logic at lines 112-117 that actually controls debug access. finding.reasoning_summary: The testharness declares jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg signals that imply intended JTAG authentication/access control, and the dmi_jtag module interface includes a jtag_key port and umode_o suggesting privilege enforcement. However, the actual MUX logic that routes JTAG DMI requests to the debug module (dm_top) uses only a simulation plusarg (jtag_enable[0]) as its guard with no check of jtag_key validity or access_ctrl_reg state. The debug CSR module internally grants DTM_SUCCESS to all requests unconditionally. This means that any agent that can set the jtag_enable plusarg or directly drive the JTAG interface can issue arbitrary debug commands (halt/resume harts, read/write memory via system bus access, execute abstract commands) with no authentication barrier.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the JTAG interface can issue arbitrary debug module commands including halting processor harts, reading/writing arbitrary physical memory via system bus access, injecting code via program buffer, and forcing the processor into machine mode via umode_o, all without any authentication. This represents a complete bypass of any software-enforced security boundaries.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal implementation of dmi_jtag that consumes jtag_key is not provided in the source snippets, so it is possible (but not evidenced) that key verification occurs inside dmi_jtag before asserting dmi_req_valid_o. Similarly, access_ctrl_reg usage elsewhere in the testharness is not shown. The finding confidence is medium because the missing guard is clear at the MUX level but the dmi_jtag internals could theoretically suppress requests on key mismatch.

finding.summary: The testmode_i/test_en signal is permanently hardwired to 1'b0 in both the testbench (ariane_testharness) and the FPGA top-level (ariane_xilinx), yet it is passed directly into the debug modules (dmi_jtag and dm_top/dm_csrs) as a test-mode enable. While the constant-zero assignment disables test mode in these contexts, the signal propagates into security-sensitive debug infrastructure (JTAG DMI and Debug Module CSRs) without any runtime gating, authentication check, or lifecycle-aware protection. The debug module is fully instantiated and accessible regardless of the test_en value, and the testmode_i pin on dmi_jtag could influence scan-chain or clock-mux behavior inside the module. In the testharness context this is a simulation artifact, but in the FPGA production context (ariane_xilinx) the same pattern applies: the debug module is unconditionally enabled with no privilege or lifecycle guard on the JTAG debug interface itself.

evidence.description: In ariane_xilinx (FPGA production target), test_en is permanently assigned 1'b0 and passed as test_mode_i to the reset generator, but the debug module is still fully instantiated and operational.

finding.reasoning_summary: The test_en/testmode_i signal is statically assigned to 1'b0 in both the testbench and FPGA top-level, which means test mode is nominally disabled. However, the debug infrastructure (dmi_jtag, dm_top, dm_csrs) is fully instantiated and active in both contexts with no authentication, lifecycle fuse, or privilege check gating access to the JTAG debug port. The testmode_i=0 assignment only disables scan-chain test mode; it does not restrict JTAG debug access. Any actor with physical JTAG access can halt harts, execute abstract commands, read/write memory via system bus access, and manipulate the program buffer — all without any authentication. In the FPGA production target this is a concrete security gap.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical JTAG access to the FPGA board can fully control the processor core (halt, resume, memory read/write, code injection via program buffer) with no authentication or lifecycle protection. This enables complete system compromise including extraction of secrets from memory and arbitrary code execution.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of dmi_jtag.sv is not shown; it is possible that jtag_key (seen connected in SNIP-0013) implements some form of authentication inside dmi_jtag, which would reduce the severity. The dm_csrs.sv body beyond the port declarations is not shown, so any internal testmode_i gating is not visible. The serpent_peripherals context receives testmode_i from an external port rather than a hardcoded value, so its security posture depends on the parent module.

finding.summary: rom2 'secure_reg' (containing cryptographic keys) is writable by any bus master without privilege or permission checks, and the reset path copies raw key material from ROM into registers exposed via the peripheral bus.

evidence.description: In rom2, the always_ff block at line 40 allows any requester with req_i=1 and we_i=1 to overwrite secure_reg entries. There is no privilege check, no lock, and no authentication guard before writing to secure_reg. Additionally, on reset (line 34), the full contents of mem (the key ROM) are copied into secure_reg unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: secure_reg holding keys is writable by any bus master and readable via rdata_o without any access control enforcement.

evidence.description: rom2 module declares 'secure_reg' as output logic [3:0][191:0], described as holding 'all the keys'. This is the sensitive resource that lacks access control.
evidence.supports_claim: The resource is explicitly named and sized as a key store, making unguarded write and reset-copy access a concrete security violation.

evidence.description: rom2 is instantiated in ariane_peripherals with rst_ni connected directly to the peripheral reset, and secure_reg is exposed as key_reg_out to the peripheral bus without any intermediate access control logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The key register output is directly wired to the peripheral subsystem with no intervening permission gate.

evidence.description: ariane_peripherals module instantiates rom2_fuse as an AXI_BUS.in slave port. There is no visible privilege or permission register in the module interface that would gate access to the rom2 instance.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: the peripheral module exposes rom2 over AXI with no access control layer shown in the interface or instantiation.

evidence.description: ndmreset_n drives the reset of the AXI clock converter for DRAM and also propagates as rst_ni to ariane_peripherals (and then to rom2). ndmreset is a debug-triggered reset, meaning a debug reset event causes rom2 to copy keys back into secure_reg (line 34 of rom2), re-exposing key material.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug-triggered reset (ndmreset_n) causes re-initialization of secure_reg from ROM, which can be exploited to re-expose or reset key state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rom2 module stores cryptographic keys in 'secure_reg'. On reset (driven by ndmreset_n, a debug-controllable signal), it copies all key material from the internal mem array into secure_reg. The write path (lines 39-41) has no privilege check: any AXI bus master that can reach the rom2_fuse slave port can overwrite key registers. The output 'secure_reg' (as key_reg_out) is wired directly to the peripheral subsystem. No access control, lock register, or privilege gate is present in either rom2 or ariane_peripherals for this resource.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged bus master can overwrite cryptographic keys in secure_reg. A debug reset (ndmreset) can be used to re-initialize key material from ROM, potentially resetting protection state. The keys are also readable via rdata_o without access control, enabling key exfiltration.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full AXI interconnect permission matrix is not shown; it is possible that an upstream crossbar restricts access to the rom2_fuse port to privileged masters only. However, no such guard is visible in any provided snippet.

finding.summary: AXI b_user and r_user fields for the DRAM master port are hardwired to zero ('0), eliminating any user-sideband privilege/security metadata that the interconnect or downstream agents might rely on for access-control decisions.

evidence.description: Lines 449-450 unconditionally assign master[ariane_soc::DRAM].r_user and master[ariane_soc::DRAM].b_user to '0 in the FPGA top-level. Any privilege or security information that should be carried in the AXI user field on write-response and read-data channels is permanently stripped.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI user sideband fields (b_user, r_user) intended to carry per-transaction security/privilege context are zeroed without any guard, meaning downstream consumers of these fields receive no meaningful security metadata.

evidence.description: Lines 1042-1044 zero out slave[1].aw_user, slave[1].ar_user, and slave[1].w_user for the PCIe slave port. All three request-channel user fields—which may carry privilege or protection attributes—are discarded before being forwarded into the SoC interconnect.
evidence.supports_claim: Request-side AXI user fields carrying privilege/security context are silently cleared for PCIe-originated transactions, bypassing any protection enforcement that depends on those fields.

evidence.description: Lines 778-779 and 782-784: when SPI is not included (InclSPI=0), the stub always asserts RESP_SLVERR but also hard-codes spi.b_user='0 and returns r_data='hdeadbeef with no access check. Any master that probes this port receives a fixed, unauthenticated data pattern instead of a properly gated error.
evidence.supports_claim: The disabled-peripheral stub returns a recognisable sentinel value (0xdeadbeef) and zeroed user field on every read, confirming no privilege guard protects the response path.

evidence.description: Lines 967-968 and 971-973: identical pattern for the disabled Ethernet stub—b_user='0 and r_data='hdeadbeef are returned unconditionally to any requester with no privilege or authentication check.
evidence.supports_claim: Disabled peripheral stubs expose a fixed data response without any access-control guard, consistent with missing permission enforcement on those AXI ports.

evidence.description: rom2 is described as holding 'all the keys' (secure_reg output of type [3:0][191:0]). Its reset is driven by ndmreset_n, which originates from the debug module, meaning a debug-triggered reset directly controls the key-holding ROM's reset line.
evidence.supports_claim: The key-holding ROM is reachable via the same AXI fabric whose user/privilege fields are zeroed, and its reset is tied to an ndmreset signal from the debug subsystem, creating a pathway where debug activity can affect the protected resource.

evidence.description: The rom2 instance is instantiated inside ariane_peripherals with rst_ni connected directly to the top-level rst_ni (which is ndmreset_n from the debug module), and its secure_reg output is wired out as key_reg_out with no access-control gate visible in the snippet.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material from rom2 is exposed on an output bus without any observed gating by privilege level or lock register.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain shows two categories of permission-related weakness. First, AXI user sideband fields (b_user, r_user, aw_user, ar_user, w_user) that carry per-transaction privilege or security context are unconditionally zeroed at the DRAM master port and the PCIe slave port (SNIP-0003, SNIP-0004). This means no downstream agent or firewall that inspects these fields can make correct access-control decisions. Second, the disabled-peripheral stubs for SPI and Ethernet (SNIP-0008, SNIP-0009) return a fixed sentinel data value (0xdeadbeef) and zeroed user field to any requester without any privilege check; the only response signal is RESP_SLVERR, which a master may choose to ignore. Together, these demonstrate missing guard logic on AXI sideband security fields and unauthenticated stub responses. The key-holding rom2 (SNIP-0005, SNIP-0007) is part of the same fabric and its reset is controlled by the debug module's ndmreset_n, adding a lifecycle concern.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or attacker-controlled master can transact on the AXI fabric with zeroed user/privilege fields, bypassing any protection that relies on those fields. Disabled peripheral stubs leak a recognizable data pattern and accept transactions from any master. If ndmreset_n is asserted (e.g., via a debug reset), the key-holding ROM's state is affected, potentially enabling key extraction or corruption via the debug interface.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual consumer of the AXI user fields (e.g., a firewall or MPU) is not shown in the provided snippets, so the precise enforcement mechanism that is bypassed cannot be fully confirmed from source alone. Similarly, the full contents of rom2.sv beyond line 16 are not provided, so whether secure_reg is gated before reaching the output is unknown. The chain context does not include the gpio port or ariane_soc package definitions that would clarify whether any other protection layer exists.

finding.summary: The debug module's dmstatus register unconditionally reports 'authenticated = 1' with no authentication mechanism implemented, and the JTAG debug interface is enabled based solely on a simulation plusarg with no hardware authentication or privilege guard, allowing unrestricted debug access to halt/resume harts and read/write memory.

evidence.description: Line 171 unconditionally sets dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1 with a comment explicitly stating 'no authentication implemented'. This means any JTAG debugger connecting to the system is always considered authenticated, bypassing any authentication requirement defined in the RISC-V Debug Specification.
evidence.supports_claim: Authentication check is permanently bypassed; the debug module always reports authenticated=1 regardless of any external authentication state.

evidence.description: The jtag_enable signal is derived purely from a simulation plusarg ($value$plusargs). The debug request mux (debug_req_valid, debug_req, etc.) is controlled only by this runtime argument, with no hardware-level authentication, privilege, or lifecycle guard gating JTAG DMI access.
evidence.supports_claim: No hardware authentication or authorization gate exists on the path from JTAG input to debug module DMI interface.

evidence.description: The dmcontrol register controls haltreq_o and resumereq_o for all harts, as well as ndmreset_o for non-debug-module reset. These are critical system control signals reachable via the unauthenticated DMI interface.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive hart control signals (halt, resume, system reset) are accessible through the debug interface that has no authentication guard.

evidence.description: The dm_csrs module exposes full debug CSR access including abstract commands, program buffer writes, and system bus access (sbaddress_o, sbdata_o) through the DMI interface, all reachable without authentication.
evidence.supports_claim: The entire debug CSR space including memory read/write via system bus is accessible with no authentication requirement.

evidence.description: The FPGA top-level instantiates dmi_jtag with no authentication port or fuse-based disable, directly connecting JTAG to the debug module.
evidence.supports_claim: The FPGA production design also has no hardware authentication on the JTAG debug path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dm_csrs module explicitly hard-codes dmstatus.authenticated to 1'b1 with a comment confirming no authentication is implemented. Combined with the testharness using a simple simulation plusarg to enable JTAG (no hardware lock), the entire debug infrastructure — including halt/resume control, abstract commands, program buffer execution, and system bus memory access — is available to any attacker with JTAG access without any authentication challenge. This is a concrete missing-authentication vulnerability on a security-critical debug interface.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical JTAG access can halt any hart, inject arbitrary code via the program buffer, read and write arbitrary memory via the system bus access interface, and trigger a non-debug-module reset, all without any authentication. This represents full system compromise from the debug interface.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The FPGA top-level snippet (SNIP-0001) is truncated and does not show the full dmi_jtag port list; it is possible but unlikely that an authentication port exists and is connected elsewhere. The testharness jtag_key port seen in SNIP-0009 is connected to dmi_jtag but the dm_csrs module itself ignores authentication state, so even if jtag_key provides some DTM-level gate, the DM always reports authenticated=1.

finding.summary: The debug module (dm_csrs) hardcodes dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1, meaning authentication is always reported as successful with no actual authentication mechanism implemented. Any entity with access to the DMI interface can fully control the debug module without any credential check.

evidence.description: Line 171 unconditionally sets dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1 with a comment 'no authentication implemented'. This means the debug module always reports itself as authenticated, bypassing any authentication requirement.
evidence.supports_claim: Authentication is hardwired to always pass, allowing unrestricted access to all debug operations including halting harts, reading/writing memory, and issuing abstract commands.

evidence.description: The dm_csrs module controls critical debug operations: haltreq_o, resumereq_o, cmd_valid_o, ndmreset_o, progbuf_o, and data_o. These are all accessible without any authentication gate.
evidence.supports_claim: All debug-controlled resources (hart halt/resume, abstract commands, program buffer, system reset) are reachable without authentication.

evidence.description: In the testharness, debug_req_valid is muxed between JTAG and DMI based on a plusargs-controlled jtag_enable signal. The debug interface entry point is controllable and routed directly to the dm_csrs module.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface is reachable from simulation/testbench and the mux selection is controlled by a runtime plusarg with no additional protection.

evidence.description: The output multiplexer directly drives haltreq_o and resumereq_o from dmcontrol_q fields without any check of authentication status, confirming no guard exists between the always-authenticated state and privileged debug operations.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard between the authenticated=1 state and the execution of privileged debug commands.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dm_csrs module explicitly sets dmstatus.authenticated = 1'b1 unconditionally (with a comment acknowledging no authentication is implemented). Per the RISC-V Debug Specification, when authenticated is 0, the debugger should have no access to debug resources. By hardwiring it to 1, the entire authentication protection layer is bypassed. Any DMI requester — reachable via JTAG or direct DMI path as shown in the testharness — gains full unrestricted access to halt/resume harts, execute abstract commands, write program buffers, and trigger non-debug-module resets.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the JTAG or DMI interface can fully control all harts (halt, resume, read/write registers and memory via abstract commands), write arbitrary code into the program buffer and execute it on harts, and trigger system-wide non-debug-module resets — all without any authentication. This represents complete debug security bypass.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain does not include evidence of whether the physical JTAG/DMI interface is exposed in the production FPGA build (ariane_xilinx.sv only shows signal declarations, not the full instantiation and connection logic). However, the authentication bypass in dm_csrs.sv is unambiguous in the source code.

finding.summary: The privilege-level check for CSR access is deliberately bypassed for CSR_MEPC: a non-machine-mode caller can read or write the machine-mode exception program counter (mepc) without triggering an ILLEGAL_INSTR exception, violating the RISC-V privilege model.

evidence.description: Line 854 contains an explicit carve-out in the privilege-level enforcement gate: `if ((riscv::priv_lvl_t'(priv_lvl_o & csr_addr.csr_decode.priv_lvl) != csr_addr.csr_decode.priv_lvl) && !(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC))`. The second conjunct `!(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC)` means that when the accessed CSR is MEPC the privilege-level mismatch is silently suppressed and no illegal-instruction exception is raised, allowing a supervisor or user-mode instruction to access a machine-mode CSR.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing privilege guard for CSR_MEPC access allows unprivileged software to read or modify the machine exception PC register.

evidence.description: CSR_MEPC (line 220: `riscv::CSR_MEPC: csr_rdata = mepc_q`) is a machine-mode register whose read path is reached via the generic `csr_read_process` block without any independent privilege guard inside the read block itself; protection relies entirely on the exception raised in `exception_ctrl`, which is now bypassed for this address.
evidence.supports_claim: mepc_q is a machine-mode CSR that should only be accessible at privilege level M; the bypassed guard leaves it unprotected.

evidence.description: Line 938: `assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q;` shows that priv_lvl_o can reflect S or U mode during normal operation, making the bypass in SNIP-0014 reachable by unprivileged callers.
evidence.supports_claim: A non-M-mode caller can trigger the carve-out path, confirming the bypass is reachable.

evidence.description: The `csr_op_logic` block (line 737-748) unconditionally sets `csr_we=1` and `csr_read=1` for most operations before the privilege check in `exception_ctrl` is evaluated; write data flows through without privilege filtering specific to MEPC.
evidence.supports_claim: Write and read enables are asserted before privilege validation, so the bypassed privilege check for MEPC allows the write or read to complete.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0014 (line 854) introduces a deliberate exception to the generic privilege-level enforcement: any CSR access that would normally raise ILLEGAL_INSTR due to insufficient privilege is let through when the target is CSR_MEPC. Because the read-path (SNIP-0002) and the write-path have no independent machine-mode guard for mepc, a supervisor-mode or even user-mode instruction can successfully read or overwrite the machine exception return address. This is inconsistent with the RISC-V ISA specification, which requires machine-mode privilege to access MEPC, and could allow privilege escalation or disruption of M-mode exception handling.
finding.security_impact: A supervisor or user-mode attacker can read MEPC (leaking the machine-mode return address) or write MEPC (redirecting M-mode exception returns to an arbitrary address), potentially enabling privilege escalation or bypassing M-mode exception handling.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The write-path case statement for CSR_MEPC is not shown in the provided snippets, so it is not confirmed whether a matching write entry exists; however the bypass in the privilege check covers both `csr_we` and `csr_read` paths equally, and the read path for MEPC is visible. The comment in the code does not explain the rationale for the bypass, but from the code alone the security violation is clear.

finding.summary: The privilege-level check for CSR access is intentionally bypassed for CSR_MEPC: a lower-privilege caller can read or write mEPC without the required machine-mode privilege, because the guard explicitly excludes that address from the privilege check.

evidence.description: Line 854 reads: `if ((riscv::priv_lvl_t'(priv_lvl_o & csr_addr.csr_decode.priv_lvl) != csr_addr.csr_decode.priv_lvl) && !(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC))`. The `&&` condition means that when the accessed CSR is CSR_MEPC, the entire privilege-level check is skipped regardless of the current privilege level, allowing any privilege level to access this machine-mode register without triggering an ILLEGAL_INSTR exception.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard that enforces privilege-level requirements for CSR access is bypassed for CSR_MEPC, constituting a missing privilege check.

evidence.description: CSR_MEPC (line 220: `riscv::CSR_MEPC: csr_rdata = mepc_q`) is a machine-mode CSR that stores the exception program counter. It is readable and writable and should only be accessible from M-mode.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR_MEPC is a sensitive machine-mode register whose read/write operations are directly exposed to lower privilege levels due to the missing guard.

evidence.description: The CSR operation select logic sets `csr_we=1` and `csr_read=1` by default, meaning that any CSR operation will trigger the privilege check path in the exception controller. Without the bypass exemption, CSR_MEPC access from S-mode or U-mode would raise ILLEGAL_INSTR.
evidence.supports_claim: Normal CSR accesses flow through the privilege check; the explicit bypass for CSR_MEPC at line 854 is the sole reason lower-privilege accesses are permitted.

evidence.description: Line 938: `assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q;` shows that outside debug mode and when umode_i is not set, `priv_lvl_o` reflects the actual (potentially lower) privilege level, confirming that S-mode or U-mode requests could reach the CSR_MEPC bypass.
evidence.supports_claim: Lower-privilege execution is possible when not in debug mode or umode_i, making the CSR_MEPC privilege bypass exploitable.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0014 contains a deliberate hole in the CSR privilege guard: the condition `!(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC)` causes the entire privilege-level mismatch check to be skipped when the target CSR is MEPC. This means that a supervisor-mode (or even user-mode) instruction can read or write CSR_MEPC without generating an illegal instruction exception. MEPC is a machine-mode register (bits [11:10] = 2'b11) and must only be accessible from M-mode per the RISC-V specification. The bypass is not present in the original Ariane reference implementation and appears to be a deliberate but incorrect modification.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with supervisor or user privilege can read the saved machine-mode exception PC (information disclosure of kernel/hypervisor return addresses) and, if writable, can redirect machine-mode exception returns to arbitrary addresses, enabling privilege escalation or control-flow hijacking from within a lower privilege domain.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The write path (csr_update always_comb block) for CSR_MEPC is not included in the snippets, so it cannot be confirmed whether a write from S/U-mode would also succeed without a separate write-privilege check. However, the read guard bypass is conclusively shown in SNIP-0014. The intention behind the bypass (possibly a bug-fix attempt) is unknown.

finding.summary: The `priv_lvl_o` output unconditionally grants Machine-mode (PRIV_LVL_M) privilege whenever `umode_i` is asserted, regardless of the actual privilege level stored in `priv_lvl_q`. This means any external signal asserting `umode_i` can silently elevate privilege to M-mode, bypassing the normal privilege-level tracking and protection.

evidence.description: Line 938: `assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q;` — the `umode_i` input is ORed with `debug_mode_q` to unconditionally force `priv_lvl_o` to M-mode. The signal name `umode_i` suggests it is a user-mode indicator, yet asserting it grants full machine-mode privilege, which is the inverse of what the name implies. There is no guard or permission check on this signal.
evidence.supports_claim: An external or improperly controlled input `umode_i` can escalate privilege to PRIV_LVL_M without any authorization check, authentication, or lock mechanism.

evidence.description: The same line also shows that `en_translation_o` (line 948) is gated on `priv_lvl_o != riscv::PRIV_LVL_M`. If `umode_i` forces `priv_lvl_o` to M-mode, virtual-memory translation is disabled, bypassing MMU protection for load/store operations.
evidence.supports_claim: Forcing priv_lvl_o to M-mode via umode_i disables address translation, undermining memory isolation enforced by the MMU.

evidence.description: With `priv_lvl_o` forced to M-mode and `en_translation_o` disabled, a process operating in user or supervisor context can access physical memory directly without page-table enforcement, and may access M-mode-only CSRs and resources.
evidence.supports_claim: Privilege escalation to M-mode and MMU bypass allow unauthorized memory and CSR access.

finding.reasoning_summary: The RTL contains a single combinational assignment that merges `debug_mode_q` and `umode_i` into an OR expression to select M-mode privilege. While `debug_mode_q` is a legitimate debug-mode indicator with controlled entry paths (SNIP-0010, SNIP-0011), `umode_i` is an external port input with no visible guard logic in the provided snippets. The name 'umode_i' semantically suggests user-mode, but it is used to grant the highest privilege level (M-mode). This inverted and unguarded use can constitute a privilege-escalation path. finding.summary: Privilege level check for CSR_MEPC is explicitly bypassed, allowing lower-privilege code to access the machine-mode exception program counter register without proper privilege enforcement.

The appended `&& !(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC)` defeats this for mepc.

evidence.supports_claim: CSR_MEPC is a sensitive machine-mode resource that should only be accessible at M-privilege level; the bypass removes this protection.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0013 contains a concrete code-level bypass of the privilege enforcement check specifically for CSR_MEPC. The condition `&& !(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC)` was appended to the privilege violation detection logic, meaning that any privilege level (S or U mode) can read or write CSR_MEPC without receiving an ILLEGAL_INSTR exception. CSR_MEPC (Machine Exception Program Counter) is a machine-mode register that controls the return address after handling a machine-mode trap. Allowing unprivileged write access enables privilege escalation (by redirecting mret to attacker-controlled code), and unprivileged read access leaks machine-mode execution context.
finding.security_impact: A supervisor or user mode attacker can write arbitrary values to CSR_MEPC, redirecting machine-mode exception returns to attacker-controlled addresses, enabling privilege escalation to M-mode. evidence.supports_claim: Any privilege level can access CSR_MEPC without a privilege violation exception, bypassing the standard M-mode-only access restriction.

evidence.description: CSR_MEPC (line 220: `riscv::CSR_MEPC: csr_rdata = mepc_q;`) is a machine-mode CSR that should only be accessible at M-mode privilege level. It holds the program counter saved on machine-mode trap entry.

evidence.description: Line 938: `assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q;` shows that privilege level output is correctly derived from state, but the exception carved out for CSR_MEPC in the guard logic nullifies this protection for that specific register.

finding.reasoning_summary: The standard RISC-V privilege specification requires that M-mode CSRs such as MEPC can only be accessed from M-mode. The guard logic in `exception_ctrl` (SNIP-0014, line 854) was modified from the standard privilege check to add a special case: `&& !(csr_addr.address==riscv::CSR_MEPC)`. This means that if the CSR being accessed is MEPC, the privilege level check is completely skipped, and no illegal instruction exception is generated. This allows lower-privilege software (S-mode or U-mode) to read or write the machine exception PC register without any fault, which is a clear privilege bypass vulnerability.
finding.security_impact: Lower-privileged software (supervisor or user mode) can read the machine exception return address (MEPC), leaking information about M-mode execution state, or can overwrite it to redirect M-mode exception returns to an attacker-controlled address, potentially enabling privilege escalation or control-flow hijacking at M-mode privilege level.

finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical JTAG access can halt all CPU harts, trigger a non-debug-module system reset (ndmreset), resume halted harts, and activate the full debug module — all without any credential. finding.summary: The JTAG DMI password check (`pass_chk`) is trivially bypassable: `pass_chk` is a combinational register that is never reset or stored persistently, and the password comparison logic is flawed — the variable `pass` is declared but never assigned a secret value (the assignment `assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE` is commented out), leaving the password as 0x00000000 (default). Additionally, `pass_chk` is set inside a combinational `always_comb` block and never stored in a flip-flop, meaning it reverts to 0 every cycle unless re-evaluated, yet `umode_o` is driven to 1 whenever `pass_chk == 1'b1` in the same combinational block. This allows an attacker to set `umode_o` (which elevates processor privilege to machine mode) by submitting any data equal to the uninitialized/zero `pass` value.

evidence.description: The `pass` variable is declared as `logic [31:0] data_d, data_q, pass` but is never driven by any assignment in the synthesizable logic. The only assignment (`assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE`) is commented out (line 88), leaving `pass` at its default value of zero.
evidence.supports_claim: The password reference value defaults to zero, making the password check trivially satisfiable with data value 0.

evidence.description: Lines 110 and 114-119 show the password check: a DTM_PASS operation sets `pass_chk = 1'b1` if `data_d == pass`. Since `pass` is zero and `pass_chk` is a combinational signal without a flip-flop, any attacker submitting data=0 via JTAG DMI can satisfy the check.
evidence.supports_claim: Password guard is ineffective due to zero-valued secret and combinational (non-persistent) storage of pass_chk.

evidence.description: Lines 177-182: `umode_o` is set to 1 whenever `pass_chk == 1'b1` in the combinational block. Since `pass_chk` is never stored in a flip-flop (not present in the always_ff block at lines 212-225), its value depends on the combinational evaluation of the current cycle. The privilege escalation signal `umode_o` is directly driven by this defective check.
evidence.supports_claim: An unauthenticated JTAG user can assert umode_o (machine-mode privilege) by submitting data=0 in a DTM_PASS operation.

evidence.description: The always_ff reset block (lines 212-225) resets dr_q, state_q, address_q, data_q, and error_q on trst_ni deassertion, but does NOT reset pass_chk. This means pass_chk has no defined reset value and is purely combinational.
evidence.supports_claim: pass_chk has no persistent or reset-safe storage, undermining any intended security enforcement.

evidence.supports_claim: Successful exploitation elevates processor privilege to machine mode, bypassing any user-mode isolation.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag module implements a password-based gate on JTAG DMI read access (DTM_READ is gated on pass_chk==1). The password check compares incoming data against `pass`, but `pass` is never assigned any value in synthesizable logic (the constant assignment is commented out), so it defaults to zero. The check `pass_chk = 1'b1` is set in a purely combinational block and is never stored in a flip-flop, so it has no persistence. Submitting data=0 with a DTM_PASS op satisfies the check in one cycle, causing `umode_o` to be asserted, which propagates to the CPU privilege control signal `ariane_umode`.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can trivially assert the machine-mode privilege signal (`umode_o` / `ariane_umode`) by sending a DTM_PASS operation with data=0, bypassing any intended access control on the debug interface and escalating CPU privilege to machine mode.

The comment on line 88 confirms the password was intentionally set to a constant but was disabled, likely accidentally left in an insecure state.

finding.summary: The JTAG password check (pass_chk) is broken: the DTM_PASS operation sets pass_chk=1 and then immediately overwrites state_d=Idle in the same combinational block, and pass_chk itself is never latched. Because pass_chk is a combinational signal that is only transiently set and never stored in a flip-flop, the READ guard at line 110 (`(pass_chk == 1'b1)`) can never be persistently satisfied across clock cycles, yet an attacker who manipulates the combinational path within a single cycle can potentially bypass the check. More critically, the write path (DTM_WRITE, line 112) has NO pass_chk guard at all — any JTAG user can issue a write to the debug module interface without ever supplying a password.

evidence.description: pass_chk is declared as a pure combinational logic register (line 79: `logic pass_chk`) and is assigned inside an always_comb block (line 117). It is never captured in a flip-flop, so its value cannot persist across clock cycles. The reset block (lines 212-226) does not include pass_chk.
evidence.supports_claim: The password-unlocked state cannot persist; the guard is effectively stateless and non-sticky.

evidence.description: At line 110, DTM_READ is gated by `(pass_chk == 1'b1)`, providing an attempted access control. However, DTM_WRITE at line 112 has no pass_chk check: `else if ((dm::dtm_op_t'(dmi.op) == dm::DTM_WRITE))` transitions to Write state unconditionally.

evidence.description: Lines 114-120 show the DTM_PASS handler: it sets pass_chk=1 (line 117) but then unconditionally overwrites state_d=Idle (line 119) after also setting it to Read (line 115). finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag module implements a password scheme intended to gate READ access via pass_chk. However, (1) pass_chk is a combinational signal never stored in a flip-flop, so the unlocked state cannot persist across cycles; (2) the WRITE path has no password guard at all, allowing any JTAG user to write to the debug module interface without authentication; (3) umode_o (machine-mode privilege output) is controlled by this non-persistent combinational signal. The combination results in a security scheme that is both bypassable (for writes) and non-functional (for reads, since unlocked state resets every cycle).
finding.security_impact: An unauthenticated JTAG attacker can issue arbitrary DMI write requests to the debug module interface (e.g., halt CPU, modify registers, access memory) without providing the correct password. This gives full debug control over the processor. Additionally, the privilege escalation signal umode_o cannot be reliably set by legitimate users due to the non-persistent pass_chk, while the write-path bypass remains fully exploitable.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full dmi_cdc.sv implementation is not shown beyond the port declarations, but the CDC bridge appears to be a transparent relay. The exact semantics of DTM_PASS and whether there is an external mechanism to latch pass_chk outside the shown code is unknown, but the always_ff block clearly does not include pass_chk among the registered signals.

finding.summary: The JTAG password check (`pass_chk`) in `dmi_jtag` is a combinational signal that is not registered, allowing it to be set and immediately consumed within the same always_comb block without persistent storage. Furthermore, the password comparison uses a combinational register (`pass`) that is declared alongside `data_d`/`data_q` but the actual value of `pass` is never assigned in the shown code (the assignment `assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE` is commented out), meaning `pass` defaults to 0. Any JTAG operation sending `data == 0` (a DTM_PASS with data=0) will pass the check and set `pass_chk=1'b1`, then `umode_o=1'b1`, which elevates the processor to machine mode.

evidence.description: Line 88 shows `//assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE;` is commented out, meaning `pass` has no defined value. The password check at line 116 `if (data_d == pass)` compares against an undriven/zero signal, making the check trivially bypassable by sending data=0.

finding.reasoning_summary: The `dmi_jtag` module implements a custom JTAG password authentication to control `umode_o` (processor privilege mode). The reference password value `pass` has its assignment commented out (`//assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE`), leaving `pass` as an undriven wire that resolves to 0. The actual `jtag_key` input port, despite being declared, is not used in the comparison — the check is `data_d == pass` not `data_d == jtag_key`. Any DTM_PASS operation with data=0 trivially satisfies the check, setting `pass_chk=1'b1` and subsequently `umode_o=1'b1`, which elevates the processor to machine mode without valid authentication.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can send a single DTM_PASS request with data=0 to bypass the password check entirely, setting `umode_o=1'b1` and elevating the connected processor core to machine (privileged) mode. This completely undermines the privilege separation enforced by the authentication mechanism.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full register-transfer behavior of `pass_chk` across clock cycles is not entirely clear from the combinational block alone — if `pass_chk` is purely combinational it resets each cycle, but the structural evidence of the commented-out password and unused `jtag_key` is unambiguous. finding.summary: The JTAG authentication `pass_chk` flag in `dmi_jtag` is a combinational variable that is never registered, allowing it to be bypassed or reset between operations; additionally, `pass` (the secret key) is only loaded during reset (active-low `rst_ni` assertion), meaning any reset event re-loads the key from the external `jtag_key` input, and `dmi_rst_no` is trivially tied to `rst_ni` without involving any authentication state.

evidence.description: The `pass_chk` variable is declared as a plain `logic` inside `always_comb` (line 79, used at lines 110, 116-118, 177). It is assigned within a combinational block: when `DTM_PASS` op is issued, `pass_chk` is set to 1 if `data_d == pass`, but then `state_d` is set back to Idle in the same combinational evaluation. Because `pass_chk` is not a registered flip-flop, it does not persist between JTAG transactions — it resets to its default (undriven/X or 0) every time the combinational block re-evaluates without a matching DTM_PASS operation.
evidence.supports_claim: The authentication gate `pass_chk` is purely combinational and not retained across clock cycles, meaning any subsequent DMI read operation that does not include a concurrent DTM_PASS match will see `pass_chk == 0`, effectively making the read guard non-functional across transactions.

evidence.description: The `pass` register is loaded from the external input `jtag_key` only when `~rst_ni` is high (i.e., during reset). This means: (1) the secret is sourced directly from an external pin/signal every time reset is asserted, and (2) any reset event — including debug-triggered `ndmreset` — causes the secret to be re-latched from the external input, which an attacker controlling `jtag_key` could exploit.
evidence.supports_claim: The password value used for JTAG authentication is loaded from an external input on every reset, creating a dependency on the integrity of `jtag_key` at reset time.

evidence.description: Line 43: `assign dmi_rst_no = rst_ni;` — the hard reset output `dmi_rst_no` is directly tied to the system reset input `rst_ni` without any authentication or privilege check. Any entity that can assert system reset can also assert `dmi_rst_no`, triggering a debug module reset independently of the JTAG authentication state.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug module reset signal `dmi_rst_no` bypasses the JTAG authentication mechanism entirely, as it depends only on `rst_ni` and not on `pass_chk` or any authenticated state.

evidence.description: At line 110, the read path guard is `(pass_chk == 1'b1)`, but `pass_chk` is assigned inside the same `always_comb` block (line 117) without any flip-flop. The assignment at line 117 (`pass_chk = 1'b1`) only holds for the duration of the combinational evaluation triggered by a DTM_PASS op. For any subsequent DMI read in a different JTAG transaction, `pass_chk` will not be 1 unless DTM_PASS is simultaneously active, making the read permission guard non-persistent.
evidence.supports_claim: The read access guard is ineffective across separate JTAG transactions due to the combinational nature of `pass_chk`.

finding.reasoning_summary: The `dmi_jtag` module implements a JTAG authentication mechanism via `pass_chk` and `pass`. However, `pass_chk` is a combinational signal (not a registered flip-flop), so authentication state does not persist across JTAG transactions. `dmi_rst_no` is directly tied to `rst_ni` bypassing authentication. The secret `pass` is loaded from an external `jtag_key` input on every reset, making it dependent on external input integrity. In the FPGA build, `dmi_rst_no` is left open and `jtag_key` appears absent, further breaking the authentication chain.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can bypass the authentication check by exploiting the non-persistent `pass_chk` combinational variable, potentially gaining unauthorized debug read access. Additionally, any entity that can assert system reset can trigger `dmi_rst_no` without authentication, and the FPGA build silently drops the authentication-dependent reset output.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact RTL for `dmi_jtag_tap` and full reset value of `pass_chk` in simulation are not provided, but the combinational declaration and usage within `always_comb` is clear from SNIP-0013. The FPGA `jtag_key` connection status is inferred from absence in SNIP-0003.

finding.summary: The JTAG password check in dmi_jtag is implemented with a combinational always_comb block that assigns pass_chk as a blocking assignment inside a case statement. Because pass_chk is declared as a plain logic variable and assigned within an always_comb block without a registered latch, it resets to its default each evaluation cycle. More critically, the password comparison directly sets pass_chk = 1'b1 inside the same combinational block that also checks it (pass_chk == 1'b1 guards DTM_READ), meaning pass_chk is not retained across JTAG transactions. Additionally, the 'pass' register that holds the expected password value is declared alongside data registers and is commented out as a hardcoded constant ('//assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE'), leaving 'pass' as an undriven logic signal (X/0), so the password check trivially passes when data_d == 0 or is unpredictable. umode_o is set to 1 based solely on this broken check, granting elevated machine-mode privilege without a functioning authentication barrier.

evidence.description: The 'pass' variable is declared at line 78 alongside data registers. The comment at line 88 shows 'assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE' is commented out, leaving 'pass' with no driver. pass_chk is declared as a plain logic variable at line 79 with no reset or registered behavior.

evidence.description: Lines 177-179 show umode_o is set to 1'b1 when pass_chk == 1'b1. Since pass_chk is a combinational signal that is not retained between clock cycles and the reference password 'pass' is undriven, this privilege elevation is controlled by a broken check.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag module implements a custom password scheme to gate debug read access and control the umode_o signal (processor privilege mode). The intended design compares an incoming 32-bit password (data_d) against a reference value 'pass', and on match sets pass_chk to unlock DTM_READ operations and assert umode_o=1. However: (1) the assignment 'assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE' is commented out, leaving 'pass' as an undriven net; (2) pass_chk is a combinational logic signal in an always_comb block with no register, meaning it cannot persist authentication state across JTAG transactions; (3) the comparison data_d == pass with an undriven 'pass' will produce unpredictable or trivially-true results. This completely breaks the authentication barrier protecting debug access and machine-mode privilege elevation.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG physical access can bypass the authentication check entirely, gaining full debug module read/write access and causing umode_o to be asserted, which grants machine-mode privileges to the processor. This enables arbitrary debug operations including memory read/write, register access, and program execution control without any valid credential.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact synthesis behavior of the undriven 'pass' net depends on tool defaults (X propagation vs. 0). In simulation it will be X; in synthesis it may be tied to 0. Either way the authentication is non-functional. The full always_ff register block for pass_chk is not shown in snippets but the always_comb declaration at line 93 and the combinational assignment at line 117 confirm it is not registered.

finding.summary: The dmi_jtag module accepts a jtag_key input that is used for a pass-check to elevate privilege (set umode_o=1), but the dmi_rst_no hard-reset output is simply wired to rst_ni unconditionally, and the key-based privilege gate in dmi_jtag can be satisfied without any authentication check on the JTAG debug interface itself, allowing unauthenticated JTAG debug access to halt/resume/reset harts and issue abstract commands.

Debug commands (halt, resume, abstract commands, ndmreset) flow through regardless of whether pass_chk is satisfied.

evidence.supports_claim: The debug module unconditionally reports authenticated=1, meaning no external authentication mechanism can actually enforce access control at the debug CSR level.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag module implements a jtag_key input and a pass_chk mechanism, but this mechanism only controls a umode_o output signal and does not gate the actual JTAG DMI request path. The dmi_rst_no output is unconditionally assigned from rst_ni. Furthermore, dm_csrs hardcodes dmstatus.authenticated=1'b1, so the debug module always considers itself authenticated, defeating any external authentication check. The combination means a JTAG attacker can halt/resume harts, issue abstract commands, read/write memory via system bus access, and assert ndmreset without passing any authentication barrier.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or emulated JTAG access can fully control the processor debug interface: halt all harts, read and write all registers and memory, execute arbitrary code via the program buffer, and reset the non-debug portion of the SoC — all without any authentication. The hardcoded authenticated=1 in dmstatus and the unguarded DMI request path represent a complete bypass of the intended JTAG key protection.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact implementation of pass_chk computation and how jtag_key is compared is not fully visible in the provided snippets (only the effect on umode_o is shown). However, the critical finding — that DMI requests are not gated by pass_chk and that authenticated is hardcoded to 1 — is clearly supported by the source snippets provided.

finding.summary: The JTAG debug interface password check (pass_chk) controlling umode_o is bypassable due to a combinational logic flaw: pass_chk is set in one branch but immediately overwritten by state_d = Idle in the same always_comb block, and pass_chk is not registered, meaning it resets each evaluation cycle. Additionally, the read operation path (DTM_READ) gates access on pass_chk==1 but the write path (DTM_WRITE) has no such gate, allowing unrestricted debug write access without authentication.

evidence.description: The password check mechanism: DTM_READ path checks pass_chk==1'b1, but DTM_WRITE path (line 112-113) has no pass_chk guard at all. Any attacker can issue DTM_WRITE operations without ever authenticating.

evidence.description: Lines 114-120: In the DTM_PASS branch, pass_chk is set to 1'b1 (line 117) but then state_d is immediately set to Idle (line 119). pass_chk is a combinational wire (logic, not reg/ff), so the assignment is only valid within this always_comb evaluation. It is never latched. The always_ff block (line 212-224) does not register pass_chk. On the next clock cycle, pass_chk reverts to its default 1'b0 (line 79 declaration, no reset assignment in ff block), making the authentication permanently ineffective.
evidence.supports_claim: The pass_chk signal is never persistently stored; the password check cannot grant persistent access.

evidence.description: Lines 177-182: umode_o is driven combinationally based on pass_chk==1'b1. Since pass_chk is not registered and resets to 0 every evaluation, umode_o will always be 0 (machine mode not elevated) regardless of password submission, OR it could flicker for one cycle making the control signal unreliable.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege output umode_o is controlled by an unregistered, always-resetting combinational signal, making the privilege escalation mechanism non-functional or trivially bypassable.

evidence.description: Line 24 shows jtag_key is a 32-bit input port used as the password reference (pass). The password comparison at line 116 (data_d == pass) compares the JTAG-supplied data against jtag_key, but the result is never persistently stored due to the combinational pass_chk issue.
evidence.supports_claim: Even if the correct password is provided, the authentication result is lost before it can protect access.

evidence.description: Lines 126-145: The Read and Write state machine issues dmi_req_valid=1'b1 directly to the debug module interface (dmi_req_o). Write operations reach the debug module with no authentication check whatsoever.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug module register writes are unguarded, allowing any JTAG user to control the processor debug interface.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag module implements a password-based access control for JTAG debug operations. However, the implementation has two critical flaws: (1) pass_chk is a combinational logic signal that is never stored in a flip-flop; within the always_comb block the DTM_PASS handler sets pass_chk=1 but immediately transitions state_d back to Idle, and on the next clock evaluation pass_chk resets to 0, making authentication permanently non-sticky; (2) the DTM_WRITE path (line 112-113) has no pass_chk guard at all, meaning write operations to the debug module proceed without any authentication check. The umode_o privilege output is therefore always 0 (authentication never persists), but the debug write path is completely open to unauthenticated access.
finding.security_impact: Any entity with JTAG physical access can issue arbitrary debug module write requests (DTM_WRITE) without providing the correct password, bypassing the intended authentication gate entirely. The read path guard is also ineffective because pass_chk never persists between JTAG transactions. This allows full unauthenticated control of the RISC-V debug module interface, enabling memory reads/writes, halt, and arbitrary code execution on the target processor.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact width and reset value of pass_chk is inferred from the declaration 'logic pass_chk' with no explicit initializer and no appearance in the always_ff reset block. If there is a separate always_ff that registers pass_chk not shown in the snippet range, confidence would decrease. The pass signal assignment (line 88 shows a commented-out 'assign pass = 32'hDEADC0DE') suggests pass may be undriven or driven by jtag_key but this assignment is not shown in the provided snippets - the actual pass driving assignment is not visible.

finding.summary: The JTAG debug access password check (`pass_chk`) in `dmi_jtag` is implemented as a combinational `logic` variable that is never persistently stored in a flip-flop, making the authentication bypass trivially achievable and the protection ineffective.

Because `pass_chk` has no flip-flop backing, the assignment `umode_o = 1'b1` is only held for the single combinational evaluation where the password matches. There is no sequential element preserving this privilege escalation signal, and no registered authentication state gates ongoing debug access.

evidence.description: Lines 263-269 show `dmi_req` and `dmi_req_valid` are forwarded directly to `dmi_cdc`, which crosses to the core-side debug module. The DMI interface, controlling the processor debug module, is what an attacker gains unrestricted access to.
