finding.reasoning_summary: The `pass_chk` signal is declared as `logic` and only assigned within an `always_comb` block. It is never clocked into a flip-flop. Therefore, whenever the always_comb block is evaluated without the DTM_PASS operation with matching password, `pass_chk` returns to its default (implicitly `1'b0` or last-driven value in combinational context). The check `if ((dm::dtm_op_t'(dmi.op) == dm::DTM_READ) && (pass_chk == 1'b1))` will thus never be true in the following JTAG cycle because `pass_chk` has been cleared. Equally, the `umode_o` output toggling depends on this non-persistent signal. This means either: (1) the password protection never works (reads always blocked), or (2) the password can be trivially bypassed by submitting both DTM_PASS and DTM_READ in a single update_dr event. The overall effect is a broken authentication guard on the debug module interface.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical access to the JTAG interface can bypass the intended password authentication to read and write debug module registers (via DMI), potentially gaining full control over the processor including reading/writing memory, halting execution, and performing privilege escalation. The `umode_o` machine-mode signal is also not reliably controlled by the password mechanism.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact reset/initialization value of `pass_chk` in simulation vs. synthesis is not shown, but in any case a combinational signal not registered in always_ff cannot hold state across JTAG clock cycles. The full dmi_jtag always_ff block is partially shown (line 212 onward) but does not appear to register `pass_chk`. The `pass` signal source (the actual password value) is declared at line 78 but its initialization is not shown in the snippets; however this does not change the fundamental flaw of `pass_chk` not being registered.

finding.summary: dmi_reset_o is driven directly from dtmcs_q.dmireset without any authentication, privilege, or lifecycle guard, allowing any JTAG user who can shift the DTMCSR register to assert a DMI reset.

evidence.description: The DTMCSR data register is selected when jtag_ir_q == DTMCSR (line 183). Any JTAG actor who can shift the IR to DTMCSR and then shift DR data can write the dmireset field of dtmcs_q.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no authentication or privilege check gating access to the DTMCSR register; any JTAG connection can select and write it.

evidence.description: Line 136: 'assign dmi_reset_o = dtmcs_q.dmireset;' — dmi_reset_o is a continuously driven output derived directly from the dmireset field of the dtmcs shift register.
evidence.supports_claim: The DMI reset signal, which resets the debug module interface, is exposed as a combinatorial function of a field that any JTAG user can write.

evidence.description: Lines 138-170 show the only conditions that modify dtmcs_d: capture_dr_o (loads fixed status), shift_dr_o (shifts in td_i data including the dmireset bit), and test_logic_reset_o (resets idcode/bypass but NOT dtmcs). There is no guard checking any permission, lifecycle state, or privilege level before allowing shift-in of the dmireset field.
evidence.supports_claim: No permission guard exists between the JTAG shift path and the dmireset bit that drives dmi_reset_o.

evidence.description: Line 163: 'if (dtmcs_select_o) dtmcs_d = {td_i, dtmcs_q[31:1]};' during shift_dr_o allows the dmireset bit (bit 16 of DTMCS per RISC-V debug spec) to be written by the JTAG TDI input with no privilege or authentication check. The resulting value is immediately reflected on dmi_reset_o via the continuous assign at line 136.
evidence.supports_claim: An unauthenticated JTAG actor can assert dmi_reset_o by shifting a 1 into the dmireset bit position of DTMCSR, bypassing any higher-level protection on the debug module.

evidence.description: The TAP FSM (lines 240-278) and flip-flop reset logic (lines 227-234) reset td_o and tdo_oe_o on trst_ni deassertion, but there is no lifecycle gating (e.g., a debug-enabled signal or OTP/fuse check) applied before the DTMCSR register can be written or before dmi_reset_o can be asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: No lifecycle or debug-enable guard is present in the path from JTAG shift-in to dmi_reset_o assertion.

finding.reasoning_summary: The signal dmi_reset_o is driven by a simple continuous assignment from dtmcs_q.dmireset (line 136). The dtmcs_q register is updated from the JTAG shift path when DTMCSR is selected as the active data register. The selection logic (line 183) and shift logic (line 163) contain no authentication, privilege, lifecycle, or lock checks. Therefore, any actor with physical or logical JTAG access can assert dmi_reset_o by selecting DTMCSR via IR and shifting a value with bit dmireset=1 into DR. This constitutes a missing guard on a security-sensitive reset output.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access (physical or through a compromised debug interface) can assert dmi_reset_o at will, resetting the Debug Module Interface. This can be used to disrupt ongoing debug sessions, clear DMI error state to cover traces of unauthorized access, or repeatedly destabilize the debug subsystem as a denial-of-service against legitimate debuggers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet does not show whether a higher-level wrapper applies a debug-enable or authentication gate before exposing the JTAG pins to this TAP module. If such a gate exists upstream, the impact would be reduced. However, within this module there is concretely no guard.

finding.summary: The JTAG TAP controller in dmi_jtag_tap.sv exposes full debug access (DMI, DTMCS) through JTAG without any authentication, privilege check, or hardware access-control guard beyond the optional trst_ni reset pin. The DMI access path is directly controlled by the JTAG IR register value, which any JTAG-capable attacker can set freely.

evidence.description: The IR register select logic maps JTAG instruction codes directly to DR register enables (dmi_access_o, dtmcs_select_o, idcode_select, bypass_select) with no authentication or privilege gating. Any JTAG user who can clock in the DMIACCESS or DTMCSR instruction code gets full access to the debug module interface.
evidence.supports_claim: No authentication or access-control guard exists between the JTAG IR decode and the sensitive dmi_access_o / dtmcs_select_o outputs.

evidence.description: Lines 175-188 show the always_comb data register select block. The case statement on jtag_ir_q unconditionally asserts dmi_access_o=1 for DMIACCESS and dtmcs_select_o=1 for DTMCSR with no surrounding permission check, lifecycle state check, or any other guard signal in the sensitivity path.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: the case block asserts privileged debug enables purely based on the attacker-controlled IR value, with no conditional permission state evaluated.

evidence.description: dmi_access_o enables direct Debug Module Interface access (line 176/184). dmi_reset_o (line 136) is derived from dtmcs_q.dmireset, meaning an attacker who can shift the DTMCS register can issue a DMI reset. These are security-sensitive debug resources with no protection.
evidence.supports_claim: The DMI (debug module interface) and DMI reset are reachable via unauthenticated JTAG IR commands.

evidence.description: The TAP FSM reset path (lines 227-234, 252-319) only resets on trst_ni deassertion or TMS=1 for 5 cycles (TestLogicReset state). There is no check against a lifecycle/debug-enable register (e.g., LC_CTRL, OTP debug state) before allowing transitions into CaptureDr/ShiftDr/UpdateDr which drive dmi_access_o.
evidence.supports_claim: No lifecycle state interlock prevents the TAP FSM from entering debug-active states in a secured/production device context.

evidence.description: The combination of (1) no authentication on IR decode, (2) dmi_access_o asserted unconditionally for DMIACCESS, and (3) dmi_reset_o driven directly from dtmcs_q.dmireset (which is shiftable via JTAG) constitutes an unauthenticated debug access vulnerability. An attacker with physical or board-level JTAG access can read/write arbitrary debug registers, halt the CPU, and reset the DMI.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthenticated JTAG access grants full debug module control with no permission enforcement anywhere in the TAP logic.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag_tap module implements a standard JTAG TAP controller that directly exposes a Debug Module Interface (DMI) and a DTM Control/Status register (DTMCS) through JTAG IR instruction selection. The source code shows no access-control guard, authentication check, lifecycle state condition, or privilege level verification at any point in the IR decode path or DR shift/update path. Any entity with JTAG pin access can load the DMIACCESS instruction, enter ShiftDr/UpdateDr states, and issue arbitrary debug transactions. The dmi_reset_o output is also directly controllable by shifting the DTMCS register's dmireset field. While trst_ni provides a reset, it does not restrict access; it only resets TAP state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical JTAG access can gain full, unauthenticated control of the RISC-V debug module: halt/resume the CPU, read/write all memory and CSRs via the DMI, and issue a DMI reset (dmi_reset_o). This completely bypasses any software-level security. In a production device this would represent a critical security boundary violation.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain does not include the parent instantiation context (e.g., dmi_jtag.sv or top-level) that might gate the JTAG clock or the trst_ni signal based on a lifecycle/OTP debug-enable fuse. If such a gate exists upstream, the severity would be reduced. However, no such guard is present within dmi_jtag_tap itself.

finding.summary: The test_logic_reset_o signal is driven purely by the JTAG TAP FSM combinatorial state (TestLogicReset state) with no hardware authentication or privilege check. The TestLogicReset state resets all IR and DR registers, including resetting the IR to IDCODE and clearing dtmcs state. The JTAG reset path is entirely unauthenticated and can be triggered by any entity with physical or logical TMS access, with no lock or permission guard.

evidence.description: The TAP FSM sets test_logic_reset_o=1'b0 as default and the TestLogicReset state is entered via TMS transitions, with no authentication or privilege check gating access to the reset state.
evidence.supports_claim: test_logic_reset_o is asserted purely from the TAP FSM state driven by unauthenticated TMS input, with no permission guard.

evidence.description: At line 102-105, when test_logic_reset_o is asserted, jtag_ir_shift_d is cleared to '0' and jtag_ir_d is unconditionally reset to IDCODE. At lines 166-169, idcode_d is reset to IDCODE_VALUE and bypass_d is cleared. The dmi_reset_o signal is continuously driven from dtmcs_q.dmireset (line 136). All of these register resets occur without any privilege, lock, or lifecycle check protecting the debug path from unauthenticated reset.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset of IR, DR, and DMI-adjacent state (including dmi_reset_o derivation from dtmcs) via test_logic_reset_o is entirely unguarded by any permission mechanism.

evidence.description: The module exposes the full JTAG TAP interface (td_i, tms_i, trst_ni) with dmi_tdi_o directly wired to td_i (line 49), and the TAP FSM state machine including TestLogicReset is fully exposed to external JTAG pins with no access control.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthenticated physical JTAG access can trigger TestLogicReset which resets the debug module interface state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dmi_jtag_tap module implements a JTAG TAP controller where the TestLogicReset state is entered by holding TMS high for five TCK cycles, a standard JTAG operation requiring no authentication. When in TestLogicReset, test_logic_reset_o is asserted, which synchronously resets the IR register to IDCODE and the DR registers. There is no lifecycle gate, privilege check, or hardware lock preventing an attacker with JTAG pin access from issuing resets through this path. The dmi_reset_o output is derived directly from dtmcs_q.dmireset (also writable via JTAG), enabling an attacker to reset debug module state without any permission check.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical JTAG access can unconditionally reset the debug module interface state, including clearing the IR register (forcing IDCODE), resetting DMI control registers, and potentially triggering dmi_reset_o. This could allow denial-of-service against debug sessions, clearing of security-relevant debug state, or disruption of ongoing authenticated debug operations, all without any privilege verification.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown in the provided snippets whether the JTAG port itself is gated by a higher-level lifecycle controller (e.g., an LC or OTP-based JTAG enable). If the JTAG interface is only exposed in specific lifecycle states and always requires hardware authentication before being connected, the risk is reduced. The source snippets show only the TAP controller internals without evidence of any external permission wrapper.

finding.summary: rom2 secure_reg (containing cryptographic keys) is writable at runtime without any privilege or authentication check, and sensitive key material is exposed as a readable output port connected directly to jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg with no access control gate.

evidence.description: rom2 declares a 'secure_reg' output port holding 4 x 192-bit key values copied from internal fuse memory on reset. The memory holds AES keys, JTAG keys, and access-control bitmaps in plaintext.
evidence.supports_claim: The register stores highly sensitive key material that should be protected from unauthorized read and write.

evidence.description: In the always_ff block (lines 36-43), when req_i is asserted and we_i is high, the code performs 'secure_reg[addr_i[...]] <= wdata_i' with no privilege check, no authentication check, and no lock bit. Any bus master that can assert rom2_req and rom2_we can overwrite any key slot.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on write path allows unauthenticated overwrite of cryptographic keys.

evidence.description: secure_reg is declared as an output port (line 13) and its full 768-bit content is continuously driven out of the module unconditionally. Line 47 also exposes individual slots via rdata_o with no read access check beyond an address bounds check.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material is unconditionally readable by any observer of the output port.

evidence.description: Lines 215-217 show 'assign jtag_key = key_reg_out[1][31:0]' and 'assign access_ctrl_reg[0/1] = key_reg_out[2/3][47:0]'. These are plain continuous assignments with no masking, no privilege check, and no enable condition.
evidence.supports_claim: No guard logic exists between the secure_reg output and the downstream jtag_key / access_ctrl_reg consumers.

evidence.description: The axi2mem adapter (i_axi2rom2) connects the AXI rom2_fuse slave port directly to rom2_req, rom2_we, rom2_addr, and rom2_wdata with no intervening access filter, allowing any AXI master with access to the rom2_fuse address range to read or write keys.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI bus is the unauthenticated entry point for both reads and writes of the secure registers.

evidence.description: jtag_key (extracted from secure_reg slot 1) is passed directly into dmi_jtag as the authentication key for JTAG debug access. If an attacker overwrites secure_reg[1] via the write path, they control the expected JTAG key and can trivially authenticate.
evidence.supports_claim: Overwriting secure_reg compromises JTAG authentication and AXI access-control policy simultaneously.

evidence.description: access_ctrl_reg feeds directly into the AXI interconnect access_ctrl matrix (line 450), so overwriting secure_reg[2] or secure_reg[3] lets an attacker grant arbitrary peripheral access to any bus master.
evidence.supports_claim: Writeable secure_reg allows full bypass of the AXI peripheral access-control policy.

finding.reasoning_summary: rom2 is a security-critical module that holds AES keys, a JTAG authentication key, and access-control bitmaps in its 'secure_reg' output register. Two vulnerabilities are present: (1) The write path in the always_ff block performs no privilege, authentication, or lock check before accepting writes from the AXI bus, so any AXI master with access to the rom2_fuse address range can overwrite any key slot. (2) The entire secure_reg content is unconditionally driven out of the module as an output port and connected via plain assigns to jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg, enabling readback of all key material without any access gate. Together these allow an attacker to both read all secrets and inject arbitrary keys, defeating both JTAG authentication and AXI interconnect access control.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access to the rom2_fuse address range can (a) read all 192-bit cryptographic keys and access-control bitmaps, (b) overwrite the JTAG key to a known value and thereby authenticate as a privileged JTAG debugger, and (c) overwrite access-control registers to grant any bus master unrestricted access to all peripherals, achieving full system compromise.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The dmi_jtag module internals are not fully shown, so the exact mechanism by which jtag_key is validated is not confirmed from snippets; however, the port is explicitly labeled 'key for jtag' and the connection is direct. No additional privilege filtering between the AXI fabric and rom2_fuse is visible in the provided snippets.
